"Fractal patterns are prevalent throughout nature (e.g., in natural environments, biological systems and human physiology)\[1,2\] because they possess a number of highly desirable functional properties. In particular, the repetition of patterns at many size scales builds highly complex structures with unprecedented surface area/volume ratios. makes sense why ultraviolet blood irradiation works so well (UVBI). UVBI was developed for medical use by an engineer, Emmet K. Knott and Virgil Hancock, M.D., and was used early in the 20th century to treat many types of diseases.

Niels Ryberg Finsen, a Danish physician and scientist, is considered to be the founder of modern “phototherapy,” the technical term for treating disease using ultraviolet light. When his own health started to fail, Finsen became interested in the bacteria-destroying effects of sunlight. This led him to develop ultraviolet treatment for a form of tuberculosis that affects the skin, known as lupus vulgaris. Finsen’s UV therapy for lupus vulgaris had a 98-percent success rate1 and in 1903, he was awarded the Nobel Prize in Medicine and Physiology “in recognition of his contribution to the treatment of diseases, especially lupus vulgaris, with concentrated light radiation, whereby he has opened a new avenue for medical science

### Pj Danahey — 2015-09-01T19:11:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pj-from-massachusetts.16801/post-176177>

Hello Everyone!

My name is Pj Danahey, I'm a 26 year old entrepreneur from Pittsfield Massachusetts. I love learning about personal wellness, health, and creativity. Over the past three years through podcasts, blogs, videos, and online communities I've made and continue to make adjustments to my lifestyle to benefit myself, my family, and my business.

I stumbled upon Jack's blog through the Bulletproof podcasts, where I was blown away by both by the science he had to share and his enthusiasm for getting the information out to anyone who was willing to challenge their existing beliefs about the science of wellness. I've been a member of the facebook group since last fall, and am regularly amazed by the quantum community's passion for scientific and personal betterment.

Glad to join the forum, continue to learn, and contribute!

Best,

Pj

# Thread: Invasive yeast growth as an argument against LCHF
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/invasive-yeast-growth-as-an-argument-against-lchf.16803/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### René Borg — 2015-09-01T22:37:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/invasive-yeast-growth-as-an-argument-against-lchf.16803/post-176192>

It was interesting I saw this study used as an argument that LCHF and ketosis are dangerous allostatic states that should be avoided for any length of time: [http://www.pnas.org/content/97/25/13619](http://www.pnas.org/content/97/25/13619)

Now there seems to be a number of issues with the study:

1\. it was done on agar not humans or other test animals thus presumably the findings may not transfer
2\. since a healthy individual can create glucose in the liver, you can presumably benefit from the observed suppressive effect of glucose even on a LCHF or ketogenic diet so this may only be a problem for people with reactive hypoglycemia to LCHF (done badly)
3\. My feeling is this article is looking at a simplistic cause-and-effect relationship between low glucose and candida albicans growth which does not necessarily need to become detrimental if the host's internal and external environment is set up differently
4\. The causal link, even as listed in the article, is not with glucose depletion per se but with increased activity of the protein kinase SNF1. Other things than just glycogen depletion may cause increased activity of this protein kinase
5\. Many other causal relationships have been suggested (stress, copper deficiency, zinc etc. etc.)
6\. The true environmental causes of the candida morphogenesis cannot be explained purely at the biochemical level because it is hidden at the level of physics (currents, light etc.)

Curious to Jack and everyone else's take on this argument being used as another rod to bash LCHF as irresponsible.

### thomas — 2015-09-01T23:04:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/invasive-yeast-growth-as-an-argument-against-lchf.16803/post-176194>

I am not a man to go deep in scientific terms. However I'll use the simplest science of them all which comes from observation then analysis:
You have no choice but to do lchf for a good chunk of the year if you live at high lattitudes. that's how your biology is supposed to work.

### Inger — 2015-09-02T09:18:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/invasive-yeast-growth-as-an-argument-against-lchf.16803/post-176233>

True! I cant find any much carbs here at all... just some berries in the woods.............. what to do...
and I am doing great
But I lie down in the morning sun.... nude.. or in my bikini... and suck in the light..... on the wet grass in the morning... under that beautiful tree in the garden... and it feels like heaven
having a raw lamb ball smoothie and fermented lamb hearts for breakfast........... life is so good

### René Borg — 2015-09-02T13:31:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/invasive-yeast-growth-as-an-argument-against-lchf.16803/post-176243>

Thomas - yes well that would be a good strategy - simply pose that question. There is no need to convince me - Jack's blogs do that already and I find his view on evolution convincing. But some of the anti-LCHF brigade does not agree on the fundamental assumptions. I get the feeling they believe that since we evolved as 'opportunistic omnivores' close to the equator, they also assume we evolved in climates where fruits were generally available year-round. They do not see the connection between light imprinting food and would likely need to be taken on a long journey to accept that notion and they would see human adaptations to a cold climate as an allostatic state not a homeostatic one - i.e. they consider our 'winter metabolism' pathological and something to continue working around (again because they do not see the connection between metabolism and light hours etc.).

What I am really interested in is the ammunition to help win the battle of ideas that is taking place - reading the above my theory would be that it is not the glycogen depletion 'per se' that makes candida albicans more aggressive - but it is something else. I know we can make the right choices without understanding the underlying mechanisms (the practitioner and our patients can rest with that) but I like to understand the mechanisms for when I get taken to task by aggressive reductionist scientists. We all know how they react Inger to 'n=1' and 'personal anecdotes' (they will shout 'confirmation bias', 'naturalistic fallacy', delusion etc. etc.)

### Inger — 2015-09-02T13:55:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/invasive-yeast-growth-as-an-argument-against-lchf.16803/post-176244>

> [René Borg said:](/goto/post?id=176243)
>
> We all know how they react Inger to 'n=1' and 'personal anecdotes' (they will shout 'confirmation bias', 'naturalistic fallacy', delusion etc. etc.)

Well... Rene.. if you want try to convince them... we have all the science in Jacks blogs... especially the latest ones... tells us everything about light... and goes deep!
So go ahead...  if you want to do it.. I do not..lol I just do not have the nerve to try to convince anyone..lol
I am thinking.. who seek will find.. and if your mind is open, you will find the truth.. with the rest I just do not bother
in my life, as I travel along.. I share with people, live, if they want to hear it or not lol but I just share. They can take it or leave it. Because it is my duty to share the light. I am forever deeply thankful to Jack that he shared it with me.......

### René Borg — 2015-09-03T13:50:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/invasive-yeast-growth-as-an-argument-against-lchf.16803/post-176308>

> [Inger said:](/goto/post?id=176244)
>
> Well... Rene.. if you want try to convince them... we have all the science in Jacks blogs... especially the latest ones... tells us everything about light... and goes deep!
> So go ahead...  if you want to do it.. I do not..lol I just do not have the nerve to try to convince anyone..lol
> I am thinking.. who seek will find.. and if your mind is open, you will find the truth.. with the rest I just do not bother
> in my life, as I travel along.. I share with people, live, if they want to hear it or not lol but I just share. They can take it or leave it. Because it is my duty to share the light. I am forever deeply thankful to Jack that he shared it with me.......

It would have been convenient because the people who need convincing are doing important work in another area but sadly cannot see beyond current prejudices once they step outside that specialty. It would have been good to have influential individuals onboard with this because they will now go and spread the opposite memes to people who trust them. Thus the battle of ideas shifts a bit away. But what you said did remind me of Stephen Covey - you have to focus on what is in your circle of influence rather than your circle of concern. I've realised this case is in the circle of concern - so I'll leave it be and focus on influencing people closer to me.

# Thread: Older is Better
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/older-is-better.16806/>
**Subforum:** Meet and Greet
**Posts:** 11
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### SallyJane — 2015-09-02T02:38:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/older-is-better.16806/post-176207>

Hey there—

I'm Sally Jane Driscoll, senior copy editor on The Journal of Urology and a commercially published short story writer. Originally from New York, I've lived in Baltimore and San Jose, and am now happily settled in the Texas Hill Country.

Starting with Mark Sisson, I eventually found Dr. Jack Kruse.

My health was miserable for many years, including systemic breakdowns every two to five years for more than two decades with enlarged liver and spleen, supposedly due to a severe bout of mononucleosis. Problem was diagnosed as Epstein-Barr, chronic fatigue syndrome, even lupus. Add blinding migraines regularly every six weeks. Then an 80-year-old physician stopped looking at numbers, looked at me instead and said, "Stress."

Since then, by finagling the food I eat to find out what works, getting outside (Cali was good for that) and judicious divorces, the older I get, the healthier I am.

Avoiding meds also helps since I often have a disastrous antagonistic reaction. Fenofibrate, some statin or other, Bupap (acetaminophen/barbiturate)—each of them tried to croak me.

Those yellow or red glasses are on my nose every evening. I'm dunkin' my face and my CryoHelmet is on order. And I'm sleeping! At least when I remember to go to bed. Pop awake at four-thirty no matter what time my head hits the pillow.

My work has made a lot of what's discussed here fairly familiar to me. The difference is that many of the basic science papers I edit are focused on chopping reality into smaller and smaller pieces. It's too rare to find people (thank you, Dr. Kruse) who are integrating data instead of searching for the magic molecule.

I'll shut up now. Thanks to all of you, too, for being here.

Cheers,

Sally

# Thread: Starting the journey...
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/starting-the-journey.16807/>
**Subforum:** Meet and Greet
**Posts:** 12
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Lin Moore — 2015-09-03T05:03:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/starting-the-journey.16807/post-176293>

Hello!

I just finished reading the Epi-Paleo Rx and I'm excited to join this forum.

My background:

Early's 30's, normal BMI but trouble with gaining wt easily in midsection. I live in central Oklahoma.
Diagnosed with Hashimotos at 3 months post partum. (Now 22 months postpartum). I'm now taking synthroid and armour.

I also have been diagnosed with PCOS, insulin resistance, hypoglycemia, thyroid eye disease, receding gums, migraines, raynauds, food sensitivity/bloating ( Jack, I'm the lady in your book described looking normal one day and 2nd trimester a few hours later!)

I also have what doctors think is erythromelalgia. I'm very sensitive to temp and exercise. I have to keep my house below 72 or my hands turn red and start to burn. Certain foods will cause same reaction. I was a collegiate runner and ballet dancer, and taught dance until I was in a car wreck 7 years ago. The Erythromelalgia symptoms started about 5 years ago and then I discontinued birth control pills (wondering if it could be cause of my symptoms). The next few years I saw over 10 different doctors to find help. No clear answers... so I ended up having to go to work part time because stress also flares up my "erythromelalgia."

I was then diagnosed with dermamyositis in 2012, Hashimotos in 2014.

Environment

Home does have wifi, currently turning off at night. No smart meter. We keep the house lights off during day unless absolutely needed. We have a lot of windows.

I teach online part time and am a stay at home mom.

I'm currently working on getting the list of recommended labs in your book.

Leptin is an 8. My dr said it was "normal" but then he didn't know anything about leptin...

Thank you for letting me join the forum. I'm looking forward to starting this journey!

### lookingforanswers — 2015-09-03T18:44:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emf-hypersensitivity.16809/post-176344>

I know I am hypersensitive to EMF. When I am in areas loaded with nnEMF I swell like crazy and have histamine issues where my face turns bright red and I get extremely overheated. If I bend my head below my heart, look out! I am literally feeling like I'm on fire!

Someone who heard Jack's latest webinar said he made the statement on there that people get worse with this condition. I'm assuming he meant if you aren't doing anything about it, right? I need to believe if I continue to follow his protocols (and yes, eventually move!) I CAN improve. That has to be possible, right?!

Otherwise, I don't know how I'll be able to live in this world. I am really scared thinking about that. I took my daughter to a concert a few weeks ago and my legs swelled so badly I could barely walk. It literally hurt to bend my knees they were so swollen  nnEMF is everywhere from the grocery stores to schools to freaking gas stations! Right now I'm feeling kind of doomed and this is not a good feeling

# Thread: Winter Options/Finding my Paradise
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/winter-options-finding-my-paradise.16810/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 26
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### lookingforanswers — 2015-09-03T18:51:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/winter-options-finding-my-paradise.16810/post-176346>

I refuse to give up. Even though I know I am super sensitive to EMF and have so many health issues and even though we haven't been able to move yet, I'm super stubborn and just wont' quit  I'm trying to figure out what is in my best interest right now. With winter quickly approaching, I need to make some plans.

I have posted before about DH trying to find a job in Florida and he is still trying - again, we're stubborn and don't give up! Unfortunately he has had some really promising prospects, but nothing has worked YET. I've made some progress healthwise this summer and I really don't want to lose that over the winter.

These are the options we're considering to make sure I continue to climb this mountain of health

1\. Go to FL for a month-long stay this winter. I'll need to find a safe house/area to rent.

2\. Buy something small where I can go to FL on an "as-needed/desired" basis.

3\. Rent something in MX for a month or so.

4\. Use a hot tub at home.

There are so many pros/cons to each of these and I'm struggling to figure it all out.

We certainly can't afford much, but I was researching real estate in certain areas of FL and wow - you can get some great deals! My concern is location, EMF, etc.

Ideally of course I'd like to be close to the beach and get plenty of sunshine even in January - this may mean southern Florida, but then I run into lots of crowds/tourists and probably more EMF. I love the idea of the panhandle area because it seems much quieter, but temps - both air and ocean - are not great in the middle of winter and I don't know how much I'd actually be able to use the ocean.

I found a great house (through a friend of a friend) that I can rent in Big Cypress Park in southern FL, but it would be an hour's drive to the closest beach on Marco Island. Driving 2 hours round trip in a rental car is not good for me with all of the EMF crap in the cars now!

There is just so much to consider and think about. Any of you who have made similar decisions or have anything to share/input please chime in! I need to make some decisions fast, but the last thing I want to do is be spontaneous and make things worse.

### Jack Kruse — 2015-09-03T20:11:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/winter-options-finding-my-paradise.16810/post-176369>

Pensacola beach to Navarre was best tested area but it aint cheap. Navarre to Mobile is too especially offseason which happens Sept 8th.

### lookingforanswers — 2015-09-03T20:29:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/winter-options-finding-my-paradise.16810/post-176378>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=176369)
>
> Pensacola beach to Navarre was best tested area but it aint cheap. Navarre to Mobile is too especially offseason which happens Sept 8th.

Thanks for that info Dr Kruse. We looked into that area a bit, but my concern is whether or not it will really be warm and sunny enough in the middle of winter, say January. To complicate matters, I have major temperature issues right now. If I'm too hot or in a lot of sun, my feet swell like crazy and I get super overheated. However, if I can get in water I'm fine. Conversely, I get cold very easily and have trouble getting in cold water or even dealing with cooler temps.

We were considering something in the panhandle area and DH suggested putting in a heated saltwater pool, but we really need to keep expenses down. We did find some pretty cheap housing options, but I don't know how we could manage a pool when we're not there - unless we get a pool service, but then we're jacking costs up again that we really can't handle. It's tough to figure all of this out, but I have faith that we will.

Well yes, in some ways I agree with you and I LOVE that quote! I do want to make sure we are at least thinking this through somewhat though as it is a HUGE expense, so if I get it wrong I may not be able to just pick up and move again, ya know?! Not to mention this requires me leaving my family for at least a month which is also huge. Not having that support and social interaction will also present challenges for me.

Yes, I agree, but I need to get the money from somewhere! The bank will only give me so much  And with not working, it's just tough. Not to mention, my house/home environment is not bad. I find I do well when I'm at home. I have trouble when I'm out and about and around everyone else's cell phones, towers, wifi, etc, etc. I do fantasize about getting closer to the ocean and improving there though!

So you said you made a scouting trip first, does that mean you have now moved south?

### Jack Kruse — 2015-09-03T22:40:57+0300

### Jack Kruse — 2015-09-03T22:43:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/winter-options-finding-my-paradise.16810/post-176415>

Money is immaterial. Get where you feel better and watch how things change. Christine showed up to meet me on 7/4/2015. We spoke for maybe an hour. Within 4 weeks she was gone from Wisconsin to Alabama.........and not it was not ideal for her or her two twins 5 yrs old as single mom. She moved without knowing a sole. Did she worry about all the other crap........yep. She still did it with no complaining because her health took priority. Ask her how she is doing now.

### lookingforanswers — 2015-09-03T23:15:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/winter-options-finding-my-paradise.16810/post-176423>

I hear you. I just need to figure out where I feel better and that alone is part of the challenge - not insurmountable, but tough. With my EMF hypersensitivity it's difficult for me to travel at all - planes, long car rides - either one wears me out. I need to know wherever I'm staying is "safe," so hotels are a definite NO. I almost need to send my DH ahead of me to check on these things, which we've done in the past, but he only has so much vacation time with his job and it's about run out now.

So, it's a lot to figure out with all of my restrictions, but I plan to keep plugging away until it all clicks. I did find some wonderful people who have vacation homes they rent in the area we are looking at who offered to test them for me if I send them my EMF meters, so I'm doing that this week!

### Joe Gavin — 2015-09-04T17:22:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/winter-options-finding-my-paradise.16810/post-176476>

> [thisbirdhaswings said:](/goto/post?id=176442)
>
> You can't wait till it all clicks. You can do some due diligence, but you also gotta be willing to jump without the perfect parachute. Sometimes, procrastination masquerades as getting all your ducks in a row, when in reality, it's just that you're scared to take action. What I love about Jack, is he's got the balls to look you straight in the face and tell you you are full of shit. If you're wise, you might look in the mirror after he says that and say, yeah, I guess I am full of shit. Then resolve to stop taking the easy way out and go for it. His version of telling me I was full of shit, was his response to my objection: "I'm scared I'm going to make a mistake." He said, "You already have. So what if you make another. So you might have to move two or three times before you find the right place."
>
> As I mulled over my options, I kept coming back to that: You already made that mistake (I said to myself). So what if I make another one. I might have to move two or three times, and that's fine. Somehow, that's what I needed...to know the next move didn't have to be perfect, I just had to put the ball in play.
>
> Now that I am here, I realize I probably won't stay in this exact house forever. But I'm in motion, and I'm in a much better position to plan my next move.
>
> Even with the stress of moving, kids starting kindergarten, all of us having birthdays, grandma coming to visit, my first experience with urgent care for one of my kids (which I've heard breaks many people!) and some very scary fires near where I live last weekend, I'm doing very well overall.
>
> I can see tangible health benefits. I stopped (literally) pulling my hair out. I started that when I spent several months in Arizona 4 years ago and had a real hard time stopping. Also, an area that was chronically cracked on a foot that has an old leg bone/ankle injury is nearly completely healed. One day I thought I broke a toe after stubbing it on a rock along the beach, and it seemed to miraculously heal after a few days with lots of time immersed in the gulf waters. I was just Skyping with a good friend of mine that I've known for 7-8 years, and I asked, "You can see it (the difference in me) can't you?" And he said yes, that I look happy for the first time since he has known me. Talk about a slap in the face. But he is right...something has changed, and it's more than a physical condition. It's an inner well-being, an outlook.
>
> Maybe it's the AM light, the DHA, the water...being by the ocean...the combination, but I am not the same person I was 6 months ago, a year ago, or 3 years ago or five.
>
> Life's not "perfect"...I still get grouchy, say things that I regret, don't get all the sleep I should every night, and sometimes stay up later on these damn devices than I should. But the trajectory is clear. And I expect those things to improve as I get a bit more settled and orchestrate my routine better.
>
> You can do it do.

All of this. Every word and syllable of this is perfect.

### Lahelada — 2015-09-04T18:43:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/winter-options-finding-my-paradise.16810/post-176482>

IF you speak a little Spanish you should be able to find something in a quietish part of Mexico. I am thinking the gulf side just across the border . A lot of people keep their boats there to avoid the higher docking costs in US so you won´t be completely alienated as people are used to tourists.
Failing that The Riviera maya would be your best destination. Too expensive ? Try Chetumal on the border with Belize . Again if you speak Spanish you can make your way into Belize and go to one of the islands by bus and speedboat . Caye caulker island may not be for you as it has an electricity substation and the hum can be heard everywhere. San pedro is larger but also more expensive.

### lookingforanswers — 2015-09-08T18:56:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/winter-options-finding-my-paradise.16810/post-176768>

Appalachicola/Port St. Joe/ South of Tallahassee - These are the areas that seem promising for us right now. Any experience with them? I know they aren't specifically the ones Jack recommends, but they seem to meet all the other requirements. Every time I check into Pensacola, Navarre and the surrounding areas I see EMF, EMF, EMF. I am just much too sensitive to all of that to take the risk right now and it seems counterproductive to me to put myself in an environment that will bombard me with something I already don't tolerate.

These areas seem much less crowded and, from what I'm being told, many areas don't even have a cell signal which is perfect! I sent my Trifield meter to a vacation rental owner who is going to test their home for me and a few others that are close to them. I've met some really wonderful and amazing people through all of this and that alone has been such a blessing

I'm still a bit apprehensive about going there over winter without my family. I am going to miss them like crazy and I am a bit concerned about depression setting in being all by myself, but I guess I won't know until I give it a try. I also worry about it not being warm enough. It looks like air temps in January are in the 40's at night with a high of about 60 and ocean temps are mid 50's. I would love it to be a little warmer, but again I have to give it a try.

I don't know if I said it before or not, but the plan for now is for DH and I to go down for a week in Oct/Nov to check things out and then I'll go down for a month in January or February to really test things out and see how I do so we know for sure if it's the right move for us.

### fitness@home — 2015-09-08T20:14:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/winter-options-finding-my-paradise.16810/post-176772>

Love Apalachicola and would move in a heartbeat. Oyster capital of Florida. There is a restored Craftsman home there for sale that we have fallen in love with.

Things are tight right now but I think about this daily.

### fitness@home — 2015-09-09T16:16:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/winter-options-finding-my-paradise.16810/post-176848>

Appalachicola - retail, restaurant, some medical (hospital is located more toward Port St Joe), not sure about manufacturing, probably construction companies, obviously fishing/oystering as it is right there on the river and gulf.

Port St Joe is a larger town than Appalachicola. We were only there for a quick shopping trip at a Walmart.

On the island (St. George) really nothing other than retail/restaurant/realtors, maybe maintenance or home management companies for the people that have vacation homes and/or rentals there? We stay on St. Geroge island annually for a spring vacation. One of my fav places to eat there is the Blue Parrot and it has a camera [http://www.blueparrotsgi.com/multimedia/livecam.htm](http://www.blueparrotsgi.com/multimedia/livecam.htm)

Also like the Up the Creek Oyster Bar in Appalachicola. [http://www.yelp.com/biz/up-the-creek-raw-bar-apalachicola](http://www.yelp.com/biz/up-the-creek-raw-bar-apalachicola)

We stay here in The Plantation:

[http://www.resortvacationproperties...s/property-details/index.php?propid=7385&vr=1](http://www.resortvacationproperties.com/rvp/vacation-rentals/property-details/index.php?propid=7385&vr=1)

### Lahelada — 2015-09-09T20:01:29+0300

# Thread: Help with Labtest (high Eosinofile, and some other stuff)
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-with-labtest-high-eosinofile-and-some-other-stuff.16814/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Thunis — 2015-09-04T00:14:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-with-labtest-high-eosinofile-and-some-other-stuff.16814/post-176436>

Hello people, thanks for helping. I have some labtest that I need help with.
As you see I have too high Eosinefile, but what could this be?
And too low hemoglobin? AND too low T3? But looks like my cholesterol is better after doing a ketogenic diet. My CRP have dropped from 9 to 3 also.

I will be happy for all the help I can get. I'm going to my doctor tomorrow, is there any labtest I should get?

I am on a ketogenic diet with blood glucose around 3-4,5 always. I try to get it lower.. but its hard without full calorie restricted ketogenic diet + metformin etc. I want it around 3-3,5, in the therapeutic metabolic zone for cancer treatment. Yes.. I had a lowgrade astrocytoma removed from my frontal lobe 3 months ago. It was gfap positiv, mutation 1 and p53 negative.

Thanks!

# Thread: Collagen I vs. III and what to do about it...
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/collagen-i-vs-iii-and-what-to-do-about-it.16817/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Fitness
**Posts:** 14
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### René Borg — 2015-09-04T13:13:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/collagen-i-vs-iii-and-what-to-do-about-it.16817/post-176458>

When my clients come to be to get their motor skills 'reprogrammed' old tendonosis can be one of the most frustrating obstacles to deal with as a movement coach.

So I am interested in what the underlying physics on this site can tell us about what goes haywire when collagen III becomes more abundant in tendons after they suffer microtrauma and how it can help me advise people better rather than necesarily sending them straight to current 'fixes' for collagen degeneration (such as surgery, stem cell infusion, blood platelet injection, laser etc.).

So current research (one link below) seems to show that collagen 3 becomes more abundant in damaged tendons instead of the 'better' collagen 1 which is normally more abundant in connective tissue. The reason is speculated to be the ability to form cross-links quicker. All this is speculation at the molecular level. I wonder do we know what the more fundamental difference is between collagen I and collagen III and what aspects of the Rxes on this site may incite the body to remove the collagen III again and replace it wth the original collagen I? Can we even achieve this without a modicum of intervention (such as perhaps laser or light therapy) to support the body's own catatoxic response to this change.

Would movement reprogramming along with the principles on this site be enough for this change or do we need to go a bit more specific. Is light also a 'scalpel' for this type of tendon change? Could the change in collagen be due to constant exposure to the wrong type of vibration frequencies from the surfaces we walk on? (i.e. because we wear cushioned footwear so the body misinterprets its habitual adjustment because it cannot pick up the correct frequency from the surface it is contacting because the foot proprioceptors cannot pick up frequency of the force wave in time for the CNS to respond optimally).

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/7671527](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/7671527)

### prAna303 — 2015-09-04T13:48:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/collagen-i-vs-iii-and-what-to-do-about-it.16817/post-176459>

Hi.

What i have done to my own body and mind thru the last ten years resonates a bit with the above.

Since i had some old injuries that i managed quite well i sensed that i had to fix them before they show up as a limit, both physical and mental. It goes very well with Jacks work combined with a deep passion with some other areas.

\- I have fixed my neck, been hit by cars four times, riding bike in training and competition.

\- I have fixed my hip, a result from that neck.

\- I have fixed my elbow, connected with the above.

\- Working on the impossible according to my old doctor (25 years ago), my eyes. This one is going to take time but the change is big.

This all boils down to one simple little thing, let go!

Since my very early days i had my own master, a teacher that has been with me every day, pushing and pulling me apart, demanding my response within seconds, always ready to pull the mat away... Nature.

Light is just like Jack says, fundamental and most of us miss the point... We are built upon that, not just in a goofball kind of way but way deeper and higher. This means that we are made to use it 24/7, 365 days / year, forever as long as we live. You say something about walking and wearing shoes. Yes this is a big issue but you need to connect it deeper. The feet is optimal without any technology applied, yes any kind of protection BUT that demands that you are connected in nature the whole time. I think it is not just about magnetism, it is the light that you should be able to transfer to higher states, your palm of your hand and the sole of your feet do not get a tan, can this be a light clue?

Why do we build extra material inside of our bodies like crazy today? Is it a healing process that is going haywire? To heal is today to steal but what happens when you do not have anything to steal from because you have technology that robs you 24/7. With strange ideas on correction to our bodies we put that extra stress to points in our bodies that leak... Light.

I have taken some athletes this summer and changed their abilities just by making them think different, some at quite high levels and everywhere the science is so static, do this and that and you should see results, bang and crash they burn down. Also the rest of us is under that stress.

I feel that we need before everything else in helping others with just say, gait and posture, we need to understand what we call redox potential here. When you are constantly transfering energy badly, your brain will loose some of its most detailed systems, those who makes things vivid and sublime. Fixing a gait issue without adjusting this first only makes the object more stressed because she can not perform like we tell her to do. Get those basic three things in place and the above issue often just, do not exist anymore, why? Loose you brain and you go back to our most basic systems, survive. In that mode, your ability to walk and make others look is not important.

What i used to fix my problems was simply, tune up my potential. Learn again that the things i thought was impossible is possible, apply it to my movement and motion, forcing myself into places where i have been told that i have no chance at my age. It all comes down to each person to make the changes by themselves, we others can only plant a virus in them.

Everything is possible, just not predictable... This is where most people who works with people fail. We who learn here should accept that fact and change the world at our place where we are.

### René Borg — 2015-09-04T14:19:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/collagen-i-vs-iii-and-what-to-do-about-it.16817/post-176463>

> [prAna303 said:](/goto/post?id=176459)

The feet is optimal without any technology applied, yes any kind of protection BUT that demands that you are connected in nature the whole time. >

To heal is today to steal but what happens when you do not have anything to steal from because you have technology that robs you 24/7. With strange ideas on correction to our bodies we put that extra stress to points in our bodies that leak... Light.
>
> I have taken some athletes this summer and changed their abilities just by making them think different, some at quite high levels and everywhere the science is so static, do this and that and you should see results, bang and crash they burn down. Also the rest of us is under that stress.
>
> I feel that we need before everything else in helping others with just say, gait and posture, we need to understand what we call redox potential here. When you are constantly transfering energy badly, your brain will loose some of its most detailed systems, those who makes things vivid and sublime. Fixing a gait issue without adjusting this first only makes the object more stressed because she can not perform like we tell her to do. Get those basic three things in place and the above issue often just, do not exist anymore, why? Loose you brain and you go back to our most basic systems, survive. In that mode, your ability to walk and make others look is not important.
>
> What i used to fix my problems was simply, tune up my potential. Learn again that the things i thought was impossible is possible, apply it to my movement and motion, forcing myself into places where i have been told that i have no chance at my age. It all comes down to each person to make the changes by themselves, we others can only plant a virus in them.
>
> Everything is possible, just not predictable... This is where most people who works with people fail. We who learn here should accept that fact and change the world at our place where we are.

Hi prAna, thank you for the long and thought out reply. I definitely recognise the question of 'defensive mechanisms' going haywire. I remember an athlete from Mali once telling me he never ran in a 'highly reactive state' which meant in a state of stress where he assumed his body would overreact to further stressors. I also see again and again this 'overcompensation' especially on the foot - my favourite example the bunions and other bony deformities that are created when the big toe has been displaced or when too much tension is drawn across the ankle bone. No doubt a thicker Achilles is an ill-informed attempt to protect the body just as the bunion is a last ditch attempt to try and build 'a new big toe' where the real one has 'gone missing'.

There is no choice to reflect on your words and take my own experiments to the next level. I walk barefoot all the time, swim in the local river etc., but cannot be outside as much as I want nor do I do as much 'pure barefoot' running as I want because I live next to gritty tarmac and rocky trails - but this is something I will have to work around. Greater exposure to nature, more protective measures from and then also fix some fundamental problems I have (such as my contact lenses - cue Jack's recent post) and then continue the 'good movement'. I will see where this takes me - and if it works then it is my obligation to communicate it to future clients.

Another clue that we cannot be 'biomechanics' or 'athletics' coaches - merely 'coaches', focusing on one part of the picture is a sin of omission by default.

### Linz — 2015-09-04T16:21:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/collagen-i-vs-iii-and-what-to-do-about-it.16817/post-176470>

Rene, I see big parallels with the equine industry here. We have huge problems with tendon/ligament injuries that never regain strength properly alongside a vast increase in metabolic syndromes, joint inflammation, and many investigations showing as many as 90% of working horses with gut ulceration. We are also seeing horses dying from toxins in sycamore trees that have grown alongside their fields for many years without a problem.

Working with principles similar to Peter to boost cell redox potential I have seen many good changes in my own very low DHA herbivores (some my vets cannot understand) but I'm still treading water to stay afloat. Makes me think very hard about my own environment when keeping the animals outside in a rural place with little artificial light or food and in constant contact with the ground/only shoeing in a few cases when it can't be avoided, isn't enough here. Most work is done on natural surfaces. Providing RO water isn't a viable option - clean and cold with Cl but no Fl (and human approved levels of nitrate) is the best I can do. My biggest progress has been made by replacing all the conventional stretches and other 'biomechanics' advice with cranio-sacral treatment and very small controlled movement in place of the 'stretching'. I'm only an amateur but this very much involves down-regulation of stressed SNS - and I think real healing occurs with upreg (or perhaps just better balanced?) ANS.
My preferred artificial interventions that seem to have helped are low photon red and IR light pads/ice packs/hydro-therapy and turmeric and I would like to know more about lasers. I think you need to start by improving redox using good water, cold therapy and natural light alongside a clean DHA and cysteine- rich diet. These would all give the collagen a better chance to re-establish with proper movement.

### prAna303 — 2015-09-04T16:33:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/collagen-i-vs-iii-and-what-to-do-about-it.16817/post-176471>

Also, there is a reason why i always have my student walk for me.

Words can say somethings but if your redox is shot, the words are quite off. Walk around and you tell me many stories, even the darkest ones.

### René Borg — 2015-09-04T20:04:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/collagen-i-vs-iii-and-what-to-do-about-it.16817/post-176485>

Linz, those observations definitely point to the truth. Optimal biomechanics is obviously desirable and we can create a fairly objective measure of optimal if we define it as 'minimising unnecessary joint torque' and 'maximing the ability to harness and create force' (power). Originally, this led me to think that the sedentary development in our population, the sitting and the over-engineered footwear was the main driver of sports injuries and the decline of Western athletics since the early 1980ies.

When I first got in contact with Bruce Lipton's thesis on the primacy of the environment (rather than the primacy of the genome) it did begin to ring some silent alarm bells coupled with the fact the poor biomechanics does not account for all injuries - we see injuries in sedentary populations that should not be occurring at the minute amounts of physical exercise we see. and then there was the many who have great mechanics and are still injured or those with poor mechanics who are not. The first could be explained partly by cohort of runners who move well but are unconditioned. But then you read the descriptions of how the Kenyans live today, how the Antipodean athletes of the 1940ies to 1970ies lived and there's a pretty big red flag being waved that indicates what you see. Take the 'Flying Kiwis' of the 1960ies - minimal technology, hardworking outdoor culture, lots of barefoot walking and running, regular exposure to the elements, eating mainly local produce that was not yet industrialised agriculture, low stress levels/good work life balance and a stoic attitude to life.

Just reading the autobiographies suggest people with a much better balance between ANS/SNS before you consider anything else. They ran 100 mile weeks and were barely ever injured.

I am going to take down your points in the last line and see how best to implement it. Where have you found it easiest to obtain low photon red and IR light pads? In Ireland we have a cryochamber as well as a very famous surgeon who works with laser (Dr Patrick Leahy, one of the pioneers of key hole surgery) who seems to be doing interesting and effective work and accelerating the healing response of tissues manyfold.

### Linz — 2015-09-04T21:06:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/collagen-i-vs-iii-and-what-to-do-about-it.16817/post-176489>

My light pad came from equinelighttherapy.com for what seemed a very inflated price for a set of LED lights and some velcro. Tax and shipping to the UK didn't help. It's for animal treatment although I have used it on a few of my own injuries too. I couldn't find anything suitable here but I think there may be some German ones. Language made it difficult to research.

I have always disliked over-engineered footwear too and the synthetic over padded boots designed to keep riders' feet warm. At least we still have some leather-soled boots although they are becoming harder to find.

### Penny — 2015-09-04T21:26:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/collagen-i-vs-iii-and-what-to-do-about-it.16817/post-176491>

I "cured" my achilles tendonitis - can anyone say dehydration - with magnesium oil (mag chloride) spray twice a day like Jack told someone on a thread here eons ago - for me, it was like a miracle - I could finally walk without pain, braces, and shoes

It's important to use magnesium throughout the day for dehydration - I just stick the mag citrate powder in my ice water - with a mint sprig - and hydrate away...

I just buy it in bulk, I use so much -

### René Borg — 2015-09-06T15:10:58+0300

I have sometimes taken very hefty magnesium supplements and sometimes not and then some Epsom salt baths. Because, at the time, I was addressing this as a simple 'cause and effect' and not as a more complex picture (as I try to know) I don't think I got the results I needed but I would like to give it a try now that I have the epigenetic ducks in a row.

I definitely think EMF and bluelight exposed me to this at the same time as I ramped up barefoot running. The problem was that I was not aware of the connection until after reading 'Lights Out' prompted by a recommendation here and I was (and am) working two jobs - IT consultant during the day - and trying to run two business in the evenings. This for a long time meant being near an electronic device working over 12 hours per day. I work for a major player in the IT world and the EMF readings you get in their offices are through the roof! ### Penny — 2015-09-06T21:23:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/collagen-i-vs-iii-and-what-to-do-about-it.16817/post-176623>

I just spray it a few times on the back of my legs and rub so it gets all the way down to my heals - in the shower, I take a handful of epsom salts and rub them all over my body - even over my heart - I get no heart weirdness anymore either - I think I put around 1/2 tsp mag citrate into a glass - but the water is alkaline, iced, has apple cider vinegar and has a sprig of mint - it would be the total bomb to add potassium bicarbonate to this, but that would make it taste vomitous -

I also - when I remember - use something called Fibro Response because it has magnesium malate in it which I think makes me more energetic because I am guessing my aluminum load is probably insane... but mag malate pills are so huge, I damn near choke on them, so that's why I go with the Fibro Response - but the epsom salts make my skin quite smooth and soft - truly a no brainer IMHO

The other thing I've been doing - as I am 100% sure at this point calcium is effluxing like crazy, is spray epsom salts all over my body when I go sunbathing in the morning - I hiked for 5 hours at Yosemite this summer in the middle of the day with nothing but epsom salt spray and never even got sunburned -

# Thread: Celeste's journal
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/celestes-journal.16820/>
**Subforum:** My Optimal Journal
**Posts:** 8
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Celeste — 2015-09-04T17:23:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/celestes-journal.16820/post-176477>

Relatively new back on the forum. I am eating fairly consistently epipaleo. I've been doing two to three meals a day. One is usually seafood (shrimp, scallops, oysters or fish). A lot of times I make the seafood into a ceviche, with a little cucumber, avocado, onion or garlic, and of course lemon. The other meal is usually grass fed bison organs, meat and marrow, with a little fermented veggies. For fats at the moment I'm using coconut oil, raw butter, the marrow, avocado, occasionally olive oil and cacao butter. I try as much as possible to eat everything raw, which I've been doing over 13 years.

I've fairly consistently been wearing my blue-b lockers after dark. Also I feel it now when I eat after dark, and the next day get back on track. I need to go and buy the blackout shades for our bedroom. When I was first reading on the forum before, I had the house assessed for EMF. We corrected just about everything focusing first on the worst problems. I do have a amber bulb in each room, including the bathroom. I love the cozy feel.

Not sure why I am having new symptoms. I noticed recently my lower jaw is inflamed along the root line. Usually never have dental issues. But it is moving a long the jaw. One area becomes inflamed and then another area gets inflamed and the prior area heals. My thoughts on what it might be:
● a reaction to having the plate in my wrist removed. Body detoxing the metal?
● A reaction to supplements. Just started taking more in the last week or so, just when this reaction got intense. Starting today will cut everything but the DHA and a raw chorella I've been using to detox the titanium.
● Or it might be the alkaline water (coral source of minerals) that I buy at the water store. If the other things don't help will switch to getting a spring water delivered. I've been using that water about a month.
● Oh, and I also recently started doing tea. Usually organic green or puerh with a spoon of coconut oil and raw butter. Usually have not done caffeine, so new to me. I only do it right after my morning or lunch meals, not in between.

Okay, out to start my day. Just wanted to get this out.

### caroline — 2015-09-05T02:05:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/celestes-journal.16820/post-176510>

I have forgotten and don't have time to read back thru ..... where are you located? California?

### Celeste — 2015-09-05T06:30:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/celestes-journal.16820/post-176525>

Yes, Los Angeles, CA. In a neighborhood called Leinart Park.

### Celeste — 2015-09-06T00:17:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/celestes-journal.16820/post-176574>

So I was looking at the cell phone reception in the area where I live, from the link Inger posted on ElEva's journal. All orange.
I guess the good part is the value of our house going up. Maybe eventually we can relocate after I retire. There were areas along the central coast with no color. Eventually do you think we'll develop technology to undo the damage?

Last night I was upset as I will often ask my husband is the WiFi off, and he says yes. I took out my meter and checked and it was relatively high on the lower range (0-3 milligauss). Still on, even though he thought it was off.

Not his fault, as I'm the one who cares about this stuff. He thinks we still need it for Roku. He helped me set up my tablet to run without it.

### Celeste — 2015-09-06T22:06:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/celestes-journal.16820/post-176630>

Yesterday met a woman who is originally French, had lived in Santa Monica and now lives on an island St. Barts. Sounds like she lives an idyllic life outside a small village. I shared with her some of the few things I had learned here. She said she does feel it coming back into LA and it is hard.

At the end of the month I start a training in Ojai, a bit away from LA. Have to check out the levels there and see what difference it makes.

### Celeste — 2015-09-16T07:22:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/celestes-journal.16820/post-177220>

What is the take on caffeine here?

### Linz — 2015-09-16T11:36:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/celestes-journal.16820/post-177221>

Celeste,
Coffee mitigates some of the effects of fluoride as it contains chlorogenic acid. See the EMF Rx blog

### Celeste — 2015-09-16T16:49:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/celestes-journal.16820/post-177232>

> [Linz said:](/goto/post?id=177221)
>
> Celeste,
> Coffee mitigates some of the effects of fluoride as it contains chlorogenic acid. See the EMF Rx blog

Hi Linz,
I was thinking of using puerh tea. It has some probiotic value. Although I've been told tea does have a certain amount of fluorine in organic form. I could even make it a sun tea, steeping in the sun so still raw.
Thanks for responding. Will check out that blog.

### SpiritGuide — 2015-09-05T06:47:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/need-some-help-advice-on-resetting-sleep.16824/post-176526>

Hi,

My wife and I did 23 and me a few months ago and started following Dr. Kruse shortly after that.

It winds up my wife's "clock genes" are all wired for insomnia...

Are there any strategies to help overcome some of these natural night owl tendencies?

She's been a night owl since she was a baby so she's having problems converting to sleeping at a normal time ... 2:00am would early for her to get to bed.

We typically stay away from pharmaceutical drugs so any alternatives would be great if you have any recommendations in addition to adding seafood and cold thermogenesis for us... Thank you for your help.

### Lahelada — 2015-09-05T12:42:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/need-some-help-advice-on-resetting-sleep.16824/post-176535>

I was never a night owl but could not stay asleep. Dr K does not recommend it but I took melatonin for a week. It helped me to rebalance then. I am not one to take anything in pill form lightly so there was no danger for me to want to continue it for my convenience sake psychologically speaking. I wanted the Leptin reset to provoke healing rather than relying on a chemical aid forever and it worked

### nonchalant — 2015-09-05T16:50:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/need-some-help-advice-on-resetting-sleep.16824/post-176542>

Hi Spirit. Does she get sunshine in the morning and darkness at night?

### nicld — 2015-09-06T21:20:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/need-some-help-advice-on-resetting-sleep.16824/post-176621>

Blue blocker glasses as soon as it get dark and keep the lights low and sun right away in the morning. That has worked for me after years of crappy sleep.

### Penny — 2015-09-06T21:30:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/need-some-help-advice-on-resetting-sleep.16824/post-176624>

Her zip code is probably wired for insomnia as well...

### cantweight — 2015-09-07T06:51:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/need-some-help-advice-on-resetting-sleep.16824/post-176652>

Make her start catching the sunrise, expose eyes and skin. So important to yoke circadian rhythm. Once the sun goes down block artificial light from eyes and skin. Develop a nighttime routine, relax/unwind. No food after dark.

It shouldnt take long for gears to switch if adhered to.

### SpiritGuide — 2015-09-10T20:06:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/need-some-help-advice-on-resetting-sleep.16824/post-176936>

Hi all, thanks for the feedback... yes she has blue blocker glasses which she uses in conjunction with the f.lux app on the computer...

she doesn't really get morning light since she's not waking up very early right now....

She's taking melatonin currently just trying to jump-start a reset in her sleep, it's been working (to an extent) recently as she's been getting to bed before 2am which is an improvement over the typical 4am bedtime.

Any other advice would be fantastic... thanks!

### JanSz — 2015-09-11T01:59:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/need-some-help-advice-on-resetting-sleep.16824/post-176979>

\[ quote="SpiritGuide, post: 176936, member: 16703"\]Hi all, thanks for the feedback... yes she has blue blocker glasses which she uses in conjunction with the f.lux app on the computer...

she doesn't really get morning light since she's not waking up very early right now....

She's taking melatonin currently just trying to jump-start a reset in her sleep, it's been working (to an extent) recently as she's been getting to bed before 2am which is an improvement over the typical 4am bedtime.

Any other advice would be fantastic... thanks!\[/quote\]

**Dx:** Brain tumor (Glioma)
**Surgeries:** Brain tumor removal surgery in 2011

\------------------------------------------------------------
Lets start with adjusting waking up time first.
Going to bed comes second.

Today
sunrise in Charlotte NC --- 07:02 AM====>>>**wakeup time**
sunset 19: 38

Assume 8 hrs sleeping time, bed time =11PM
dinner finished at least 4hrs before bed time =7PM

7:40PM shut off all electronics, tv, ect

to see around use red light and black light (available at amazon or Home Depot).
candles, fire, .
\============================================
Optimize your Steroid Hormone Panel.
Hell or high water.
at least:

cortisol (levels and shape)
DHEAs
progesterone

Look what Jack is saying

[https://www.jackkruse.com/cold-thermogenesis-7/](https://www.jackkruse.com/cold-thermogenesis-7/)
These mechanisms are mediated by the hormone DHEA which is the major antioxident in the brain and correlates directly with effective sleep by lowering IL-6 levels. Progesterone is another critical hormone for brain homeostasis and learning as well. Sleep is vital to mammals, but it is supremely vital to humans, because they have shrunk the benefits of hibernation into 2 short critical hours of their sleep cycle because of the massive growth of their brains extinguished the need to sleep through the winter months.

\============================================
There is a problem with blue blockers (I think).
There is a problem with the name blue blockers.
The glasses that we are usually using are designed to block UV light.
My "blue blockers" have UV400 label on them.
By design they are meant to block UV light of 400nm down.
Any blocking of light frequency of more than 400nm is incidental.
Blocking UV400nm and down is hurtful and should be avoided.
Any glass or plastic stops UV light.
That includes lenses implanted during cataract surgery.

All above is new and just came from Jack with UBIQUITINATION #24
[https://www.jackkruse.com/ubiquitination-24-are-myopia-light-and-aging-linked/](https://www.jackkruse.com/ubiquitination-24-are-myopia-light-and-aging-linked/)

The way I read it, we need (UV-A)+(UV-B) that occurs naturally
but we also benefit from them in all other times,
see the 20 year long experience of the restaurant crew in Chicago **Sherman Hotel Well of the Sea.**

Dr Kruse in UB#24 posted picture of his daugter's bedroom with (black light)=(UV-A)+(UV-B) in it.

# Thread: 'Port wine stain' (nevus flammeus) and epigenetic questions...
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/port-wine-stain-nevus-flammeus-and-epigenetic-questions.16828/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Kids
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### René Borg — 2015-09-06T22:33:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/port-wine-stain-nevus-flammeus-and-epigenetic-questions.16828/post-176632>

My 14-week old was born with a port-wine stain birthmark on the side of his left leg. He's a healthy and contented toddler and it gives him no issues but I am still curious as to whether it can be removed/reversed and what him having this tells me about his epigenetic expression and future.

current research seems to suggest that the port wine stain is caused by a mutation in the GNAQ gene expression in the later stages of foetal development (when this mutation happens earlier it is linked to the much more serious Sturge-Weber syndrome).

The gene is responsible for many things including making a protein important for cellular signalling and skin pigmentation so my layman's understanding is that this switch basically causes some of the capillaries in the skin to develop differently.

Now what I wonder is manifold: 1) can it be reversed through the 'optimal protocols' and are any in particular important for reversing this, 2) would laser therapy be the obvious choice later on to remove it and 3) is there anything in particular that we know triggers these changes in GNAQ gene or is it the random quantized world at action - i.e. this was just how that gene got knocked around by modern environmental signals and 4) does having this mutation link to any benefits or weaknesses later in life?

### nonchalant — 2015-09-07T00:49:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/port-wine-stain-nevus-flammeus-and-epigenetic-questions.16828/post-176638>

> [René Borg said:](/goto/post?id=176632)
>
> My 14-week old was born with a port-wine stain birthmark on the side of his left leg. He's a healthy and contented toddler and it gives him no issues but I am still curious as to whether it can be removed/reversed and what him having this tells me about his epigenetic expression and future.
>
> current research seems to suggest that the port wine stain is caused by a mutation in the GNAQ gene expression in the later stages of foetal development (when this mutation happens earlier it is linked to the much more serious Sturge-Weber syndrome).
>
> The gene is responsible for many things including making a protein important for cellular signalling and skin pigmentation so my layman's understanding is that this switch basically causes some of the capillaries in the skin to develop differently.
>
> Now what I wonder is manifold: 1) can it be reversed through the 'optimal protocols' and are any in particular important for reversing this, 2) would laser therapy be the obvious choice later on to remove it and 3) is there anything in particular that we know triggers these changes in GNAQ gene or is it the random quantized world at action - i.e. this was just how that gene got knocked around by modern environmental signals and 4) does having this mutation link to any benefits or weaknesses later in life?

I am also interested in this, Rene'. A relative had several (scary for a child) laser sessions, which only reduced the strength of the stain, theoretically. I suppose it might be less vivid.

### René Borg — 2017-09-05T20:08:42+0300

It's not particularly epigenetically ideal where we live really apart from being rural and with no immediate neighbours, own waterwell and fresh air. We are in Ireland on the 53 degrees N line and our mountains are really low hills - the house lies on 350 metres with the surrounding national park going no higher than 928 metres. We do get a lot of sunshine when it's available but it's only truly good between April and September.

# Thread: The Changing Nature of Ice Cubes: EZ demonstration?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-changing-nature-of-ice-cubes-ez-demonstration.16832/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Scompy — 2015-09-08T00:39:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-changing-nature-of-ice-cubes-ez-demonstration.16832/post-176695>

Now that I'm using ice-cubes 24/7, a scientific property has occurred to me that I haven't realized my entire life until just yesterday. The nature question has to do with this method:

1\. Half-fill ice cubes in a plastic tray
2\. Freeze fully for 3 hours
3\. Top off the ice-cube tray with water
4\. Freeze fully again for another 3 hours
5\. Attempt to twist the ice cube tray to break the ice free

Expected Results: The ice cubes break free with normal pressure/force as if the ice-cubes had been filled fully the first time.

Actual Results: The ice cubes require extra pressure/force to break free when they are first half-filled and then topped off with water again.

So my question would be: Why do ice cubes in trays, when half-filled and frozen, then fully-filled later, seem to cling stronger to the sides of the ice-cube tray?

Possible explanation? Is this result have to do with EZ lattice formation?

Another question comes to mind about biohacking water... What about 1/10th filling the tray in 10-stages... would refreezing somehow increase the EZ formation because of surface-area lattice formations? Get me thinking about glaciers in nature if surface areas increase the EZ formation.

# Thread: Hack your cordless phone!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-your-cordless-phone.16834/>
**Subforum:** The EMF Rx
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Cpt.Tired — 2015-09-08T06:58:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-your-cordless-phone.16834/post-176717>

Since my wife INSISTS on having a cordless phone in the house I had to start brainstorming.

I bought an old 900Mhz cordless phone from Value Village for $3.99 since they do not transmit between calls and the handsets are approx. 1/20th less powerful during calls (I used my meter to compare). I wanted to use the current answering machine since it has some good features but stop it from transmitting.
I opened it up and removed the two little antennas with a soldering iron. (Just snipping them off at their base may work too.)
The reading went from 8 mW/meter squared down to 0.00uW/meter squared (at approx 5 feet away.)
Perfect! (The meter was a TES 593. A good meter according to Mike Neuart )
Just for reference, Dr Klinghardt wants his patients sleep location to measure no more than 5uW/meter squared.

But what really surprised me is that ....THE PHONE STILL WORKED! Even in the backyard!

Of course it's not perfect..there still is some radiation at close range (within 4 ft) and the 900Mhz phone is still better EMF wise. I would still use the speaker phone mode on the DECT phone handset.

So there's an idea for someone who lives with family members that just won't give up that thing!
Hope this helps someone..

### nicld — 2015-09-08T21:13:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-your-cordless-phone.16834/post-176781>

I just put mine on a timer to be off at night. Told the DH that it was to conserve energy. He said what if we get a call in the middle of the night. We still have a corded phone in the kitchen plus we cannot hear the phone in the bedroom.

### Penny — 2015-09-08T22:20:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-your-cordless-phone.16834/post-176792>

That was brilliant What about one of those headsets with the air tubes for whoever is still hell bent on using that type of phone?

I did something really stupid - I got what I thought was a corded phone and the EMF reading on the handset was quite hideous - but at least it doesn't act like a cell phone tower in your house -

What is the model number of your 900 mhz phone?

We got a trendnet router at Fry's for $24.00 over the weekend - totally wired and it works great -

### Cpt.Tired — 2015-09-09T02:07:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-your-cordless-phone.16834/post-176817>

> [thisbirdhaswings said:](/goto/post?id=176785)
>
> Just going through this, too. In my case, telling my neighbors I shut the phone off after sunset so if there's an emergency and I can't be reached by phone, come pound on my door.
>
> Of course, I keep a plain old fashioned phone plugged into a phone line but I don't give that number out.
>
> At the end of the day, we don't really \*need\* a phone 99.99% of the time (at night).
>
> I'm curious...I stopped pluging in/out my cordless because I started thinking when it's not plugged in but charged, it might be sending out a searching signal. Does it?

There was very little coming from the base unit/handset when I measured. At 5 ft. it was not measurable (0). When the unit is not plugged in there is nothing transmitting.
The thing is that if you want complete protection you really cannot use a cordless phone because even though the reading is zero, there still has to be something coming from it for me to be able to use the handset in the backyard.

### Cpt.Tired — 2015-09-09T02:11:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-your-cordless-phone.16834/post-176819>

Later I developed a problem adrenal (no f). Increasingly isolated, thin, ruined, arose digestive problems. Shortly after, I broke a hand bone. I had surgery (2012) and subsequently appeared fibrosis. Again operated in 2014.

I decided to change. I hugged this page in June 2014. I printed some fundamental blogs and camped. I hugged CT, drank more water, ate epi-paleo (DHA c / ketosis), bathed me in the sea, I fixed my environment, living day and sleep (bad) night.Improved. Not tolerate technology. ### nonchalant — 2015-09-09T23:04:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/some-help-w-inflammation.16839/post-176876>

Molybdenum is needed by the gut microbiome to digest sulfurous foods, as I recall. It helped me with onion and coffee problems, though I never had any big issues with other sulfurous foods like eggs, seafood, pork, and cruciferous veggies. The more sulfur we can eat, the better.

### lilly antic — 2015-09-09T23:46:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/some-help-w-inflammation.16839/post-176881>

Hello @[jmct77](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/14543/),
your homocysteine level is quite high. My lab e.g. uses the range till 10 mol/l.
the reason for a higher level could be deficiencies in B12 and folic acid ( also MTHFR SNPs).
B12 and folic acid are also cofactors in the conversion of neurotransmitters. They help convert dopamine->noradrenaline->adrenalin.
So addressing a potential deficiency in B12 and folic acid might help mitigate the anxiety issues.

### Erik — 2015-09-09T01:43:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/question-about-emf.16841/post-176812>

I want to make my point about an emf question i have...

There are two places i traveled where my acne/rosacea and digestive issues completely vanished even thoug i ate like crap. One of this places was extremely low in nnEMF. No cellpohne signal, no wifi, weak radio signal... But the other place wasnt so low in EMF, so ¿why did i have such an improvement? (there was wi-fi, some cell signal, and radio signal as well.) Well the thing is that this place was a village in a kind of a valley between two mountains FULL of trees... My question is. Even thoug there where nnEMF there, could it be that the soil (paramagnetic materials in the mountains maybe) and the trees provided a stable magnetic field, i dont know much about the topic, but could it be that so much nature was countering those nnEMF?

Thanks

### Lahelada — 2015-09-09T01:55:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/question-about-emf.16841/post-176813>

It is possible I think. Don't forget that the South Atlantic magnetic anomaly reaches to here so anything g stabilising that should be good.

### digital — 2015-09-09T23:52:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/converting-nnemf-to-ir.16843/post-176882>

[https://www.google.com/patents/US20030162007](https://www.google.com/patents/US20030162007)
**Energy converting article and method of making**
US 20030162007 A1
ABSTRACT

A graded composite energy converting article having a working surface of **carbon-bonded carbon-fiber** (CBCF) material that **can absorb light, RF, microwaves, or other forms of energy and convert this energy to sensible heat**. Conversely, CBCF can take sensible heat and convert it to radiative emissions of IR energy. Energy is conducted through the article by carbon foam to a secondary surface. The graded composite is made by first heating pitch under pressure to create the foam, which is then pyrolyzed and graphitized to create graphitic, open-cell foam. A slurry of chopped carbon fibers and organic binder in water or solvent is prepared and deposited on a working surface of the foam, preferably infiltrating the foam to some depth. The green body is dried, cured, and carbonized, creating a graded body of carbon foam in conductive thermal contact with a working surface of CBCF. Secondary surfaces of the foam may optionally be metal-infiltrated for added strength, brazability, or other purposes. The article may be used for optical absorbers, baffles, beam stops, RF and microwave loads, radiators, electron collectors, and other uses where energy is passively converted from one form to another and superior thermal transport is required.

\[0001\] **The United States Government has rights in this invention** pursuant to contract no. DE-AC05-00OR22725 between the United States Department of Energy and UT-Battelle, LLC.

### Jack Kruse — 2015-09-15T17:53:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-dont-believe-in-coincidences-biophotonics.16845/post-177163>

EVERY PHOTON COUNTS:[http://www.mpg.de/595098/pressRelease20090420](http://www.mpg.de/595098/pressRelease20090420) Came across this recently. I guess this is another proof that light comes first even in our regulation of DNA. Evolution has again found a clever way to collect light and then use it for non optical signaling within different layers of cells using Fermat's law. Heterochromatin breaks light more than euchromatin and somehow this seems to help eurkaryotic cells to be more flexible in chromosome arrangements. You may not quite understand it, but its getting more and more obvious of we how we are regulated down to the smallest part by light. The study was published in Cell in 2009 ( Irina Solovei, Moritz Kreysing, Christian Lanctot, Süleyman Kösem, Leo Peichl, Thomas Cremer, Jochen Guck, Boris Joffe
Nuclear architecture of rod photoreceptor cells adapts to vision in mammalian evolution
Cell, 17. April 2009 )

### Jack Kruse — 2015-09-15T18:04:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-dont-believe-in-coincidences-biophotonics.16845/post-177164>

SUMMARY
We show that the nuclear architecture of rod photore-
ceptor cells differs fundamentally in nocturnal and
diurnal mammals. The rods of diurnal retinas possess
the conventional architecture found in nearly all
eukaryotic cells, with most heterochromatin situated
at the nuclear periphery and euchromatin residing
toward the nuclear interior. The rods of nocturnal
retinas have a unique inverted pattern, where hetero-
chromatin localizes in the nuclear center, whereas
euchromatin, as well as nascent transcripts and
splicing machinery, line the nuclear border. The
inverted pattern forms by remodeling of the conven-
tional one during terminal differentiation of rods.
The inverted rod nuclei act as collecting lenses, and
computer simulations indicate that columns of such
nuclei channel light efficiently toward the light-
sensing rod outer segments. Comparison of the two
patterns suggests that the conventional architecture
prevails in eukaryotic nuclei because it results in more
flexible chromosome arrangements, facilitating posi-
tional regulation of nuclear functions.

### RobH — 2015-09-15T18:43:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-dont-believe-in-coincidences-biophotonics.16845/post-177168>

Reminds me of a lecture I was fortunate enough to attend at the Stanford Sierra Camp about a year and a half ago. Dr. Lubert Stryer (of the biochem textbook fame, mine from med school) gave a lecture on some of his work with rhodopsin, transducin, cGMP, etc. He interspersed it with beautiful photographs he'd taken during his travels and talked about the perception of light and how much it fascinated him. He is a scientist who had't lost touch with his right brain (trying to reconnect mine...). I remember wanting to ask him some specific questions about quantum biology at the time and refer him to your blogs, and never had the opportunity, but I wish I'd paid better attention (or remembered more) now that we've veered so significantly into light / photoreceptors / retina.

Rereading CT-7 this AM. Jack, I feel like you're a bit of a magician using sleight of hand, or maybe, probably more accurately, I'm just easily fooled and was looking the wrong direction when you introduced all this so long ago. Light was there all along in your blogs.... but it always seemed secondary to something else you were talking about .... (diet, cold, EMF (yes, I know light is EMF), hormones, tensegrity), etc. But it's always there somewhere.

It's just marvelous to reread these old blogs and see things really start to come together! Kind of like collagen organizing from gelatin when a current is applied.... 8^)

RobH

highest food source of k2 is natto - fermented Japanese soybeans - I believe they're fermented with bacillus subtillus - my favorite probiotic - you need vitamin d, k2, magnesium and boron to keep the calcium from effluxing as badly or mitigating the damage from the calcium that has landed in the soft tissue like tooth roots, arteries, kidneys - i.e., it should take the calcium from those places and stick it back into your bones -

I don't think you can hurt yourself with the following:
5000-10000 IU of vitamin d per day
5-15 mg of k2 per day (don't exceed 45 mg-see Japanese protocol for osteoperosis-they use this)
3 mg of boron
500-1000 mg of magnesium malate/citrate per day

I'm doing this myself and have had zero issues except my teeth don't hurt anymore - I am also shining a UVB/IR light in my mouth as well for 2-5 minutes at a time -

But, in the UK, you need UV light - for that, go to the pet store and get a reptile flourescent light with UVB - put it above your head about 2 feet

Seafood, seaweed is critical because in order to transduce the UV light into electrons, you need DHA - fish oil won't cut it -

### av8r — 2015-09-11T13:19:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/rf-emf-in-bedrooms.16848/post-176990>

Cover the bedroom wall with lengths of aluminum foil that the dumb meter is on. Create a z-bend between the runs of foil so shinny ends are in contact. Then for aesthetics cover over the foil with a poster collage using a theme your son is into.

### Scompy — 2015-09-11T17:34:43+0300

In theory though, my electric company is going to allow me to remove it and replace with an analog smart meter. But then I'm also thinking, it's best anyway to block off the walls from any RF regardless of the source.
