# Thread: Hello
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello.16850/>
**Subforum:** Meet and Greet
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Eddie O — 2015-09-11T04:02:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello.16850/post-176987>

Hi,

My name is Ed. I live in a small city in Vermont. I first came across while doing research on static magnetic fields and saw Jack's endorsement of the Magnetico mattress.

I'm a divorced father of 4. One of my children has health issues, so that's what has really compelled me to dive head first into this area of research.

My original passion was history. I've grown increasingly interested in science, but a science that is informed by macro-evolutionary and ecological processes. In a sense, I found the Darwinian approach to biology limited precisely because it takes a very narrow view of competition and reproduction at the expensive of all other considerations or levels of analysis. It fails to look at a wider ecosystem that underpins our shared biological and ecological matrix. That is why I find Jack's approach really appealing, particularly in terms of its rigor and its foundation in physics. It would be interesting to hear Jack's take on natural selection from a quantum perspective. I had previously read a few books that pushed me in this direction, such as Robert O. Becker and Jerry L. Tennant, but it still left much to be desired in terms of how to apply it to reversing and preventing health issues. That's where Jack and this forum come in!

I'm looking forward to becoming an active member of the forum and learning from Jack and all of you.

### fitness@home — 2015-09-11T15:45:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello.16850/post-176996>

Welcome Eddie! You should start a journal and document your child's issues. It is easier to get regular input from forum members via journal entires.

### Eddie O — 2015-09-11T22:59:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello.16850/post-177011>

Hi Fitness,

Thanks for the welcome. I'm going to start a journal after I've worked my way through the webinars and blog posts. That way, I'll have a better chance of doing it in a more systematic manner.

### Jack Kruse — 2015-09-12T16:49:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello.16850/post-177022>

Eddie Jack already wrote about natural selection.......OSF 3-5 are complete quantum ideas.

### Eddie O — 2015-09-13T06:27:34+0300

# Thread: Benefitting Children: Collecting Data on Schools
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/benefitting-children-collecting-data-on-schools.16853/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Kids
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Scompy — 2015-09-13T18:56:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/benefitting-children-collecting-data-on-schools.16853/post-177066>

I was thinking about meta-data on schools asking the questions:

1\. Do children at your school get early morning light: daily Recess?
2\. Do you have fluorescent lights in your classrooms?
3\. Do you use WiFi or other RF-EMF in your schools?

Map these three elements against the Absentee-Rate of the children in the school for an overall idea of sickness in the school. Also we could look at their grades too.

I'm wondering if getting data back from schools is possible or collectible over the internet right now.

Anyway, I'd love to get data that shows what happens to children's health and performance when you get them out into the sunlight in the morning while eliminating fluorescence (and LED) and RF.

The big question to ask is: Why can't I find a study that at least shows the difference between incandescent in one school and fluorescent lights in another school and then compare the health of the children in both schools over time? This issue reminds me of adding flouride to the water supply. Someone about 60+ years ago thought it was a good idea with very little scientific study and then it becomes status-quo. Someone thought it was a good idea to add fluorescent lights to classrooms, and then no one notices. These are all issues of 'seeing is believing.' You can't see the added flouride in your water and you can't see the interaction of light across the skin and retina, so everyone continues to go on blissfully naive. RF is another one too... unless someone has a meter to measure it.

### Scompy — 2015-09-16T22:06:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/benefitting-children-collecting-data-on-schools.16853/post-177241>

Had a great conversation at my son's school today with the school's Principal. A half-hour later talking about RF-EMF and biological intersections, she undestands enough now that she's pushing the issue forward. For her own benefit, I also showed her how her 'wired' setup on her laptop was still giving off 2 milliWatts/Sq.m because her WiFi wasn't turned off on the laptop. She was so pleased to get the information that now I'm going to be meeting with their IT-engineer soon. Fingers-crossed. One school at a time, right?

### Scompy — 2015-09-20T20:41:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/benefitting-children-collecting-data-on-schools.16853/post-177458>

The Principal talked to me again saying that she's talking with the IT person about ways to reduce the milliWatts/Sq.m of the RF at my children's school. That's a great start to say the least that they want to see about taking action. However, they need an education. Their first tactic was to move the WiFi to other locations higher up. I've already told them that moving of the WiFi may not eliminate how RF reflects off of surfaces, and actually could make the problem worse in certain locations... that meters are needed. I offered the school to have them borrow my meter so they could get better measurements overall. It will be interesting to see how this pans out in the next few weeks. At least I've got them aware, but I may need to go in and help EMF-hack their school for them.

# Thread: Leptin Rx @ Altitude?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-rx-altitude.16854/>
**Subforum:** The Leptin Rx
**Posts:** 16
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### NadoRo — 2015-09-14T04:40:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-rx-altitude.16854/post-177089>

A little background: 41 year old female, living at altitude (5,000 ft) for the last year. Moved from sea level in So Cal..not optimal, I know, but husband is active duty military and move was required.

This is my second time at the Leptin Rx in two years, and I am fully committed to seeing it through this time. I am finding, however, after 40 days on the protocol, that I have made less progress than the last time I tried it while living in Southern California. While I have lost 6 pounds and am seeing an improvement in body composition, I am not experiencing the sleep gains or improved energy I had hoped for. I have a ketone blood meter, and it's interesting to me that it is showing depressed ketone production even though I have been strict about staying below 30 grams of carbs most days.

I drink spring water, eat at least one seafood meal a day, blue blockers at sunset, and read books by a red incandescent lightbulb at night. I'm also on BHRT for low sex hormones, NDT for subclinical hypothyroidism, and supplementing DHEA & Pregnenalone to bring up those numbers. After a year of supplementation, my sex hormones are reaching respectable levels, yet my sleep is still very bad with multiple night wakings and occasional difficulty falling asleep. I have started adding in CT with ice packs or cold baths (tap water is cool in Denver, even in September) but have not diligently followed the CT protocol. My initial focus was to get the Leptin RX well underway before I tackled CT.

My question is this: Can I expect the Leptin Rx to take longer living at altitude? Is there anyone who has done the Leptin Rx (and CT too, if applicable) and can share their experience with it in this environment?

Thanks for sharing!

### Sue-UK — 2015-09-15T15:21:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-rx-altitude.16854/post-177143>

Hi

Have you read the Ubiquitination 1 blog? Lots of nuggets about what happens to cells at altitude .... so you may need to do a lot more than if you were living at sea level.

Anyway, Welcome !

Ionosphere nnEMF also is made of a myriad of waves above your head.........the sunlight light is also made of said waves. All are part of the electromagnetic spectrum. They can and do interfere and cancel out and other time they summate. This means the more densely populated area will have the ability to bury sunlight in the daytime affecting the quantum yield of photosynthesis and full spectrum sunlight to your eye and skin. This means you might live in a sunny place like LA or Orlando and never be able to make Vitamin D. Since it is fall now you Vitamin D should be at its highest level . Check your D and learn something about your current zip code.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-rx-altitude.16854/post-177204>

Implementing the CT protocol stat. Based on my reading of Ubi 1, I gathered CT would be a requirement to mitigate the effects of altitude and nnEMF here in Denver. Incidentally, I just measured Vitamin D levels in August. After a summer spent outdoors, at altitude mostly, it plummeted 30 points from 70 to 40..not good. Fortunately, we are moving to sea level in 1 1/2 years, but I'm not waiting that long to improve my health! Thank you, Dr. Kruse and Sue-UK for your insight.

### nicld — 2015-09-17T21:25:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-rx-altitude.16854/post-177288>

Welcome and I love Colorado but Denver is a nnEMF HELL HOLE for me but Red Rocks is AWESOME!!!!

Went out there in 2014 to take care of the SIL for a bit and after 3 days we needed to escape and drive into the mountains. Felt so much better there. We will probably be heading back there sometime before Christmas to get the SIL's stuff since she could no longer afford the rent out there and has had to move back by us.

### NadoRo — 2015-09-18T05:48:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-rx-altitude.16854/post-177309>

Yep, the mountains are amazing, though my sleep is even worse when we are in the ski resorts (all average 10 K and higher for elevation). The nnEMF issue aside, altitude matters.

### nicld — 2015-09-18T16:24:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-rx-altitude.16854/post-177320>

For me, I just don't get why I feel great at altitude. We went to Pike Peak and went biking down it and when we were up there I felt great. Running around, biking with the rest of the family was huffing and puffing a bit. I have never had an altitude headache like everyone else. The only time I felt the altitude was hiking at Red Rocks but I live at about 800 feet. I was in awe of the person we say riding a unicycle there.

### b612 — 2015-09-18T17:52:51+0300

### NadoRo — 2015-09-19T03:12:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-rx-altitude.16854/post-177353>

Maybe it's because I'm not accustomed to living at a higher elevation..I've lived at sea level in one state or another for the last 20 years. My physiology is likely to blame here as well. I'm starting a journal in the appropriate area for context, but came to the Leptin Rx with Adrenal Fatigue, depressed hormones, excess body fat, poor sleep..in other words, a far cry from optimal! After a year here, though, I'm surprised by how much my heart races just climbing the steps in my CO home. My husband has observed on more than one occasion that he feels better every time he leaves for a trip to a sea level location, and he's never read any of Dr. Kruse's stuff and is in better shape than I.

### Jack Kruse — 2015-09-19T17:01:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-rx-altitude.16854/post-177380>

When you are at altitude you are closer to UV light that is more abundant and it is cooler so you assimilate more UV. the issue for many is the O2 tensions are lower so you must assimilate the UV via skin and eyes. If you have clothes and sunglasses on altitude can kill you because you are effectively blocking UV from the eyes and skin. This is the basis of the Ubiquitination 1 blog post.

### NadoRo — 2015-09-21T21:40:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-rx-altitude.16854/post-177513>

I've been reading the Dehydration thread from the forum...Since moving to 5,000 feet from sea level, I've repeatedly joked that I feel permanently dehydrated living here..now I know why: low pg coupled with altitude=dehydration=poor sleep. Doubled up my progesterone last night (still in first half of cycle) to 200 mg and started face dunking per the CT Rx. Throw in a lot of conscious grounding and outdoor time yesterday, and I slept through the night for the first time in several nights..no melatonin, just a probiotic and magnesium glycinate, red glasses at sunset, and the increased progesterone. That thread is GOLD and supports what I've intuitively felt since moving here!

### thomas — 2015-09-14T14:49:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/yeched-mat.16857/post-177095>

Continuing my journal that started in meet and greet there:
[https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/yeched-mat.15266/](https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/yeched-mat.15266/)

I have spent the past week really awfully tired and depressed (my thought go crazy when I am tired, can barely function, Life really really sucks then).
Dunno what triggered it. Might be nnemf, might be bacteria/virus/parasite, or just decreasing light levels (lower and lower uv in france.)
I slept well tonight and today I am ok, back to a happier more relaxed state. To do that I went to bed around 8 pm solar time, which is as early as I 've ever gone to bed in my adult life as I remember.
Pretty sure going to bed at the same time everyday is bad advice in yearly cycles. As we approach september 21, we get to the maximum declining levels of light per day. Seems to me I need to go to bed earlier and earlier till december.

Concerning that topic I am researching:
\-How to never sleep like shit in the first place. Have consistent good sleep even if I fuck up.
\-When I sleep like shit, how to restore brain function.

\---

One thing I've found in the past month, I try to spend the first 2-3 hour of the day outdoors (eating, walking...). It really does improve my mood a lot.

\---

Seems there's a reason may-june-july were and are still my favorite months of the year.. Little clothing and uv.. feels good.

### Inger — 2015-09-14T18:51:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/yeched-mat.16857/post-177102>

I go to bed at 8.30 or 9 PM once in a while lately..... but mostly 9 to 9.30 PM... I LOVE to go to bed as soon as it gets dark/as it starts to get dark. It just feels so right.

I usually know why my sleep is disturbed.....
for me it is
too late eating...... like 7 PM is too late. I prefer nothing after 5 PM
too much nicotine/caffeine/alcohol (shame on me )
Too late work/too much stress = high cortisol I think

if I fuck up I usually do not sleep too well and that is fine when I know that. A few days or even a day of great living restores me fast tho, thankfully!
But to be honest I have had way too many sins this summer..oh man. What to do..lol

To restore crazy fast I start with morning light outside grounding.... ditching computer.... walking in the woods at sunset.... lots of water... raw ball smoothies / fish heads / wild herbs / oysters / raw heart..... lots of good stuff and raw! Always gives me energy.. especially high hearts. They are awesome! And then I eat a early dinner and go early to bed and I am new again

I think we can still enjoy the natural light, a lot  even if it gets darker.... we just need to spend time outside.. like you do.
It is funny... we all can tolerate different amounts of "cheating" and we do feel it when it is too much, no?
well.. then we have to stop..lol take a break and suck us full of energy again
I cant tolerate too much and I start to feel so awful, uh. But I do restore very fast, which is awesome
My bad is, I close to never have a day off from work, most weeks I work 7 days. So I need to be pretty strict as my life is so busy already. There is not too much time to restore, because you need time, to be outside in the wild. That is why I need to be careful not to do too much wrong.
Right now it is raining here.... but I lay on my floor grounding at my open terrace door so get natural light on my skin/eyes as I am on the computer
and tonight I will go to bed latest at 8.30 PM for sure... cant wait to go out to that silent dark horse barn.. man I love to sleep there

Kind of you to ask^
I am doing pretty well today.

I think in the beginning of the week, Got some of the best sleep and alertness of my life. Then seen some family, started to eat Raclette at 10 pm, in tons of powerfull leds/fluo lights.
That fucked me up. Took me a good 2/3 days to recover. Today fine again.
Eating after 5-6 pm = Hell.
Artificial light I just can't stand it.
I think I transformed into a morning type of person. Typical social activities in the evening are so fun, but so not worth it.
Now That I know what good sleep is like, I just search consistency.

Being outdoors most of the morning is a pretty big transformation. More and more outdoors = happier.
On that point pretty confused about living indoor/outdoors/shelter.
I know I want to be outdoors most if not all the time. (at least during daylight, but I love the stars too)
But we grow up and live in an indoors society.
I am so used to living in an house, but envisioning a lifestyle without..
Oh well..

I know I can handle fall/winter much better than a year ago. but still creating my winter Rx. still thinking about moving in between tropics when the darkest monthes come.

### Gagnrad — 2015-10-07T15:31:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/yeched-mat.16857/post-178807>

> [thomas said:](/goto/post?id=178757)
>
> thanks guys^ it's fun to make catchy sentances isn't it.

Well, I was soundly beaten, Thomas, as mine was only from a rock song.

It was the first line of Cream's _Tales of Brave Ulysses_. I think _he_ was in my mind because I've been re-reading the _Odyssey_ - or rather listening to it as an audio book this time. I'd just got up to the encounter with the cyclops. I found it quite interesting to listen to, because I'd forgotten that the cyclopes were said not to till barley, which just grows wild around them, and to "have no civility". They're "wild men" of a sort - though they keep sheep.

What I thought was interesting was that they're solitary. It made me think of the 17th and 18th century contract thinkers, who envisaged the "original state" of man, whether positively or negatively, as a solitary one. Nothing could be further from the truth, of course. And a human being in a truly solitary state wouldn't even be recognisably human - as your countryman Jean Marc Gaspard Itard found with Victor of Aveyron (_L'Enfant Sauvage_).

[https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jean\_Marc\_Gaspard\_Itard](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jean_Marc_Gaspard_Itard)

### thomas — 2016-01-19T13:54:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/yeched-mat.16857/post-186728>

It's now clear to me, the time of the year I feel best is late August/ September. There I can think the best, feel happiest, get the most motivation.
I think it's also the time when Vitamin D is the highest(in mid-latittude-north-hemishpere), and when I got the most accumululated sun. Coincidence? I think not.

If we consider that 40-50deg solar angle is the minimum to make significant amount of vitamin D, then we need to go south of 16-26deg lattitude to make D all year long.(north hemisphere).

### thomas — 2016-01-29T15:19:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/yeched-mat.16857/post-187611>

This morning I got a few ideas to cure some of the mental clutter that plague me from time to time...
Comparing yourself to others, or to most people = Conformity = The most dangerous force = Fastlane to mediocrity = Guaranteed mental clutter
You have your own path among an infinite number, Every one has his own unique path. Some people achieve in a week what others won't even do in their lifetime. the only difference is what's in between their ears and how they use it.
Don't compare yourself to others, instead take mindsets, ideas and behavioral cues from people that inspire you.

### thomas — 2017-12-14T21:44:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/yeched-mat.16857/post-230321>

So I just returned from 3 weeks in fuerteventura. This island got a great energy. Lots of volcanoes, lots of sun, lots of ocean and waves. Cool people. Not too many wifi&cell antenas, I barely used internet there. hell lots of areas without signal even. I can definitely recommand the canaries for the european people, easy and cheap to go, rather inexpensive too.
Other than that I have peroids where I follow the health principles of this community better and others where I just fall off (to the point of living late at night, sometimes).

On another topic.

A bit on this model:

(some inspiration)

peace(of mind). thomas.

# Thread: Cravings - Glycogen Stores Used Up?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cravings-glycogen-stores-used-up.16859/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Huck — 2015-09-14T19:04:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cravings-glycogen-stores-used-up.16859/post-177103>

Please tell me if this makes sense.

I realize there can be different reasons for having cravings, but I'm wondering if there might be one primary reason - glucose. Let me use this analogy I found on [wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Glycogen):

> **Glycogen depletion and endurance exercise**
> Long-distance athletes, such as [marathon](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Marathon_%28sport%29) runners, [cross-country skiers](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cross-country_skiing), and [cyclists](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cyclist), often experience glycogen depletion, where almost all of the athlete's glycogen stores are depleted after long periods of exertion without enough energy consumption. This phenomenon is referred to as "[hitting the wall](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hitting_the_wall)".
>
> Glycogen depletion can be forestalled in three possible ways. **First**, during exercise, carbohydrates with the highest possible rate of conversion to blood glucose (high [glycemic index](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Glycemic_index)) are ingested continuously. The best possible outcome of this strategy replaces about 35% of glucose consumed at heart rates above about 80% of maximum. **Second**, through endurance training adaptations and specialized regimens (e.g. fasted low-intensity endurance training), the body can condition type I muscle fibers to improve both fuel use efficiency and workload capacity to **increase the percentage of fatty acids used as fuel,[\[12\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Glycogen#cite_note-12)[\[13\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Glycogen#cite_note-13)\[_[citation needed](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed)_\] sparing carbohydrate use from all sources. Third,** by consuming large quantities of carbohydrates after depleting glycogen stores as a result of exercise or diet, the body can increase storage capacity of intramuscular glycogen stores.[\[14\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Glycogen#cite_note-14)[\[15\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Glycogen#cite_note-15)[\[16\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Glycogen#cite_note-16) This process is known as [carbohydrate loading](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Carbohydrate_loading). In general, glycemic index of carbohydrate source does not matter since muscular insulin sensitivity is increased as a result of temporary glycogen depletion.[\[17\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Glycogen#cite_note-17)[\[18\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Glycogen#cite_note-18)
>
> When experiencing glycogen debt, athletes often experience extreme [fatigue](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fatigue_%28physical%29) to the point that it is difficult to move. As a reference, the very best professional cyclists in the world will usually finish a 4- to 5-hr stage race right at the limit of glycogen depletion using the first three strategies.
>
> When athletes ingest both carbohydrate and [caffeine](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Caffeine) following exhaustive exercise, their glycogen is replenished more rapidly.[\[19\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Glycogen#cite_note-19)[\[20\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Glycogen#cite_note-20)\[_[unreliable medical source?](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Identifying_reliable_sources_%28medicine%29)_\][\[21\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Glycogen#cite_note-21)

So is that what happens when we get cravings - we "hit the wall"? I'm thinking that if we are on a low carb diet, but not in ketosis, we don't have quite enough glucose to handle all our body's needs. Eventually the glycogen stores in muscles and liver get used up and our body tells us it needs glucose - NOW. So we get cravings and fill up on ice cream, cookies, chocolate, potato chips, etc. We're good for a couple of weeks until the same thing happens again.

Comparing to the endurance athlete from above, this is similar to third - consuming large quantities of carbs after glycogen stores are depleted. Some people avoid cravings by increasing carbs and never getting into ketosis (First). But it looks to me that getting into ketosis (Second) is the option we want and it should eliminate cravings.

Is this right? All these years all I had to do was decrease my protein and increase my fat and I wouldn't have cravings? If it is that simple, I could have reached goal weight 30 years ago!

### Penny — 2015-09-14T21:33:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cravings-glycogen-stores-used-up.16859/post-177114>

you might look into the wonders of glycine... don't remember a hell of a lot about that particular rabbit hole, but I think it helps with gluconeogenesis - like when you wake up in the middle of the night because you're out of sugar - it kicks in - I could be remembering this totally wrong though - sometimes I take it before bed (1/4 tsp) and it helps with sleep - have 50 grams of carbs divided into morning and lunch - get it in the form of greens/cabbage/broccoli - it also spikes superoxide -

### Huck — 2015-09-15T22:42:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cravings-glycogen-stores-used-up.16859/post-177208>

This post should be labeled **Fat Guy on Lawnmower!**

Thinking is my favorite hobby, but for most of the last 20 years, I couldn't seem to keep a thought in my head long enough to do much thinking. For the last 2 1/2 weeks I have been eating lots of fat and the brain is starting to fire. I spent about 3 hours on my lawn tractor cutting the grass, in the sun wearing just a pair of shorts and actually did some thinking. Probably faulty, as I'm out of practice.

Back to the glucose question. Assuming a climate that experiences a cold winter, would it make sense for ancestral man to crave carbs in the middle of winter? No, because there would be very limited carbs available. Would it make sense for him to crave carbs in the summer or fall? And when I say crave, I'm talking about those that make you get up and drive to the store at 10 pm for some ice cream. So would ancestral man get cravings in summer and fall and why?

I can think of 2 possible reasons:

1.  He's not eating enough carbs and his body is warning him that there is not enough glucose for his organs and his muscles. Eat some cabs now.
2.  He's not storing enough fat for the winter. Eat some carbs now.

From this, I made some assumptions:

-   Ancestral man ate a ketogenic diet with limited carbs part of the year (cold/dark) and ate a higher carb diet (warm/light) the rest of the year.
-   When on a ketogenic diet, there was no reason for the body to crave carbs, because carbs would throw him out of ketosis - he was using ketones for fuel and burning body fat

-   When on a higher carb diet, it made sense to crave carbs, because his body required high levels of glucose to both function and store fat.

So I believe the answer is, if one is in ketosis, one will not crave carbs.

### Penny — 2015-09-15T23:57:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cravings-glycogen-stores-used-up.16859/post-177210>

One needs to add the cold to emulate evolution - plus, that lawn mower of your is sending much EMF to your body - just like a motorcycle would... so, embrace the cold and skip the lawn mower

# Thread: nnEMF changes our quantum yield to give us brain disorders

**Subforum:** Educating Doctors
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jack Kruse — 2015-09-15T18:08:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nnemf-changes-our-quantum-yield-to-give-us-brain-disorders.16862/post-177166>

Modern living could be responsible for an unprecedented increase in worldwide cases of neurological brain diseases. Colin Pritchard, from Bournemouth University (United Kingdom), and colleagues compared 21 Western countries between 1989 and 2010 and found that dementias are starting a decade earlier than they used to in adults. Furthermore deaths caused by neurological disease have risen significantly in adults ages 55 to 74 years, and for adults ages 75+ the rate has virtually doubled in every Western country in just the last 20 years. The team notes that in the US, neurological deaths in male over 75s have nearly trebled and females rose more than five-fold. For the first time since records began, more elderly US women died of brain disease than cancer. The study authors warn that: “Every country's neurological deaths rose relative to the controls, especially in the USA, which is a cause for concern and suggests possible environmental influences.

[http://www.worldhealth.net/news/neurology-related-deaths-cause-concern/](http://www.worldhealth.net/news/neurology-related-deaths-cause-concern/)

### Sean L. — 2016-11-02T19:34:36+0200

Reading an article linked to by Robb Wolf analyzing the benefits of aerobic fitness. Read the quote below and decide what really caused this woman's stroke. The writer seems to think it was a combination of lack of exercise, constant traveling and eating airport food that led her to the ICU. Man, the diet and exercise paradigm can be truly blinding to the glaringly obvious quantum fundamentals at work. All factors sure, but I think only JK followers would appreciate the oversight on the true 'health risk' here.

"I had just talked to my mom the night before and nothing was wrong. In general, she seemed healthy: she didn’t have any pre-existing medical conditions, she wasn’t on any medications, she wasn’t overweight, she didn’t drink, and she didn’t smoke.

Of course like the majority of people, she didn’t work out — and her diet definitely could have been better. But she was a flight attendant and was very active, routinely walking all over the place during her layovers. She was on one of those layovers in Miami when she had the stroke."

[http://www.8weeksout.com/2016/11/01/why-conditioning-may-save-your-life/](http://www.8weeksout.com/2016/11/01/why-conditioning-may-save-your-life/)

### smith5 — 2016-11-06T18:49:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/want-to-bet-that-this-astronaut-is-wrong-on-his-feeling.16863/post-203665>

You never mention altered gravity as a concomitant condition for rapid bacteria growth in space except for nnEMF.

# Thread: Wim Hof Method Online Training Course
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/wim-hof-method-online-training-course.16869/>
**Subforum:** Biohacking 101
**Posts:** 8
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### E_than — 2015-09-17T00:17:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/wim-hof-method-online-training-course.16869/post-177246>

Hi there,

I am not sure which thread is the best thread for this question so I am posting it here and in the CT section. I am wondering if anyone has done Wim Hof's online training course and if so, was it worth the $?

Thanks,

Ethan

### Jerry Fulford — 2017-03-20T00:14:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/wim-hof-method-online-training-course.16869/post-210702>

I'm new here, and have been scrolling through the back pages to learn. I have taken the course, but have also been practicing various gTummo and pranayama exercises, along with old water exposure for years. Worth the time and energy to learn.

### Jack Kruse — 2017-03-20T03:22:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/wim-hof-method-online-training-course.16869/post-210716>

I think you can find enough data on line to do it yourself. The prices some people have paid seem high to me. In vivo training I think is worth it but more expensive.

### Nathan — 2017-03-20T03:28:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/wim-hof-method-online-training-course.16869/post-210720>

I purchased the course and it was worth it to me. Sign up on his site and a few times a year he has sales and I think I paid $129. Just get started with the breathing and if you like it and want to learn more then get the course. Wim covers others things in the course like how to stretch deeper and some yoga moves and you can ask questions as well.

### bionaut — 2017-03-20T04:04:01+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/wim-hof-method-online-training-course.16869/post-210724>

I waited for the course to go on sale. Got it half price. I think it is way more effective in cold environments. I am in a warm place now so I do not use it as much. It was a catalyst In getting me to explore different types of meditation and learn how to apply them, so it was worth it.

### Jerry Fulford — 2017-03-20T12:53:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/wim-hof-method-online-training-course.16869/post-210738>

Yes, much info online for free to get one started.

bionaut- You can still use the breathing method in warm areas. You're just not using cold exposure. It works with heat.

### bionaut — 2017-03-20T14:32:34+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/wim-hof-method-online-training-course.16869/post-210745>

> [Jerry Fulford said:](/goto/post?id=210738)
>
> Yes, much info online for free to get one started.
>
> bionaut- You can still use the breathing method in warm areas. You're just not using cold exposure. It works with heat.

Thanks, I know the technique can be used in heat. What I mean is I have done them in hot and cold environments and it is awesome when it is cold out. My intuition is that this type of breathing is affecting synthesis or usage of melatonin or a similar compound in the brain, so I would expect a cold dark environment to be supportive of its use.

### Penny — 2017-03-20T18:17:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/wim-hof-method-online-training-course.16869/post-210758>

youtube has a bunch of demonstrations - I used it right before jumping into the cold ocean - it helped

# Thread: Diary(I mean Journal) of a Madman
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/diary-i-mean-journal-of-a-madman.16871/>
**Subforum:** My Optimal Journal
**Posts:** 118
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### FutureVision — 2015-09-17T02:17:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/diary-i-mean-journal-of-a-madman.16871/post-177254>

Dear Diary...uh...Journal,
I'm a mad. Not because I've worked in marketing for a number of years(Get it? Madmen?) Or that I like Ozzy's music(hence the title). I'm mad because for a number of years I've been trying to fix my health and despite listening to the experts in health and fitness I haven't made the progress I have expected. Sure, I've put on some muscle and some of the symptoms I face have cleared up a bit or at least have gotten better at times but I can't help but face the fact that as a 42 year old I should not be waking up feeling like crap and be stiff, achy and overweight with the changes I have made. So let's start there. What do I think are my issues?

1.  Psoriasis - It's not severe(at least to the level I have seen some people have it) but I have anywhere from dime sized to slightly bigger than a silver dollar patches on various parts of my body. Arms, leg, scalp, tail-bone and torso to name a few. I have had it for roughly 18 years.
2.  Weight - At 6'2" I weight in right now at about 227. Much more muscular than I have been in the past but still overweight. It's been hard to get down to my ideal weight which I look at as 15% body fat and was the initial reason why I started looking at naturopathic and functional medicine.
3.  Cognitive function(ADD, depression, and anxiety) - You'll see a number of supplements that I have recently tried to deal with this more naturally. Before that I tried Citalopram for 6 months starting last summer and added in gerneic amphetimine salt. Quit both shortly after and tried amino acid therapy and adaptogenic herbs and a quality source of choline.
4.  Inflammation - This manifests in a number of ways. Bursitis in my hip(diagnosed in May 2013). I get some edema in my hands and in my shins. I get what I feel is tendinitis occasionally in wrists and shoulders. And I most likely have some in got some my lower back(and probably everywhere else) I guess we can even lump the psoriasis and cognitive function in with this one too. Had my appendix removed in May 2013 which was probably also related to inflammation.
5.  Lower back - Typically stiff and the source of most of my stiffness and even pain in the morning and occasionally through the day. I suspect it is tied with my next issue
6.  Numbness in right shin - So this is relatively new. My right shin feels numb. My right side is also the side with the tight hip. I use a standing desk most of the time so I'm not sure if that may be tied in in any way but I try and make a conscious effort to keep my spine organized(a la Kelly Starret) and I feel that has helped some along with stretching.
7.  Malasma - At least that is what it looks like. At first appeared on the left side of my neck but now there is line across my forehead. This is very recent. Within the last month.

Time for a little history

**You are where you are**
Grew up on Long Island and lived there until the age of 16 although when I was 1 or 2 years old that I lived in Florida for a year or so. Senior year of high school moved to Florida and was there until late 2006 when we then moved to southern New Hampshire. Been in NH ever since. May have had a small stint on Mars but that is unconfirmed (jk).

**Get up offa that thing**
Let's talk about my activity through the ages. I played sports most of my youth. I don't recall exactly what age I started soccer or baseball but I was pretty young. Played both sports all the way through high school on both recreational and scholastic teams. Went to college and didn't try out for the teams(shame on me) but I did pick up street basketball a couple of years later on a fairly regular basis along with a smattering of other activities like rollerblading and flag football. I don't recall exactly which year it was but after a hiatus with the sports got out on the basketball court and tore my meniscus and partially tore my ACL on a spin move and that pretty much put the kibosh on sports for me although I do recall bracing up the knee and trying to QB in flag football.

All through college I had fairly active jobs or at least jobs that kept me on my feet. I stocked shelves and worked fast food for part of it. My longest stint was delivering and assembling RTA furniture before I moved into working temp customer service positions before landing a permanent job job as a graphic designer in mid 1999. It was the beginning of a career and the acceleration of a decline in health. Don't remember what year it was but I knew I was getting heavier and started working out. Went for a job one morning and for the rest of the day it felt like my heart kept racing intermittently. Visit to the cardiologist showed an enlarged right ventricle(probably around 2004-2005). Did all the testing to reproduce an arrhythmia but they couldn't. Seemed like things were stable. I topped out around 252lbs at this time and started to take my eating and exercise a little more seriously. I did drop some of the weight(haven't been back to that weight since) but I believe I was still around or above a 25 BMI. Moved to NH and one of the first things I did was get a cardiologist. We did more scans and I wore a Holter monitor for 24 hours. All tests and scans showed no signs of the enlarged ventricle. Apparently eating better and exercising healed it up.

Ok. Now let's get a little more detailed. Over the last 7 or 8 years I've gotten a lot more serious in my health. When I started a new job in 2007 I took advantage of the gym memberships benefits offered and would be at the gym in the early morning before work. Around the spring of 2008 I decided to get really anal about CICO and tracked everything I ate on a Google doc(I'll share those if you want to see them). I wore a heart rate monitor for 24 hours and track total caloric burn for a 24 hour period to get a baseline. Then each day I would add caloric burn for my workout minus the baseline caloric burn for that would have happened if I did not workout. Then I tracked everything I consumed for the day including things like creamer in my coffee. I always aimed for 1500 calorie deficit but was happy if I only hit a 1000 calorie deficit. I dropped 20 lbs doing this but that weight came back in short order when I didn't stick my routine. Late Fall 2008 we started an 11 week Biggest Loser contest at work with a cash prize. I can't recall how much I lost over the 11 weeks but it was over 20lbs. I lost my job before the contest was over in Jan 2009 but they were cool(or felt sorry for me) and let me come in and do my last weigh-in as a non-employee. I weighed in at 199 and won the cash prize(99% positive that it was earned and not consolation prize for losing my job). 199 was the lowest weight I had been since college. Job gone and with it the gym membership. Put on 13 lbs over the next few months but started P90X as a replacement for the gym time. I had noticeable gains in muscle mass compared to the weight lifting and chronic cardio I was doing when I dropped to 199. No real weight loss though but that was expected if I was putting on muscle. Eventually got a new job and got really bored with P90X(and frustrated by some of the workouts). Took up going to a local gym before work on a daily basis and at one point got back down to 211 but haven't seen that weight since. To sum up those years between 2009 and 2012 it was a lot of doing the same thing. Get on a workout program for a length of time and try to eat clean and then gain it all back when I got burnt out on working out and/or dieting. Life events would often derail me too.

I believe it was 2012 when my quest intensified. Paleo was front and center for me and I was reading a lot more about intensity over duration when it came to training. I started a workout program called Athlean-x which is focused on shorter intense workouts and workouts were never identical except the challenges which were designed to see how much you improved over a month long period. I tried frequent meals again this program suggested. No real weight loss but I did see overall strength and stamina gains plus it wasn't boring. After doing the 3 month program a few times over the last few years I tried switching to a strictly heavy lifting program with just compound movements like squats, deadlifts, benchpress etc... I definitely lost on the stamina edge but I felt like I gained some strength. Tried to take up road biking on the off days but that hasn't been successful. Then, early this summer, I switched to alternating weeks of heavy lifting and the functional training in Athlean-X up until a few weeks ago where I ditched all exercise to do the Leptin reset.

A typical workday does involve getting up and moving around a throughout the day. I also work at a standing desk much of the day and try to keep a good posture doing so and even try and get in some stretching mainly for my hips.

I'd say that is pretty much all to my activity history.

**If you don't eat yer meat, you can't have any pudding. How can you
have any pudding if you don't eat yer meat?**
Time for food talk. I grew up in a household where my mom was pretty on top of giving us what she believed was best for us. Breakfast was often cereal or oatmeal. She tended to avoid the more sugary cereals if I remember correctly and go with Cheerios. Eggs and sausage(and buttered rolls fresh from the bakery) were a typical Sunday morning breakfast. School lunch was often a sandwich(PB&J, Tune, _Braunschweiger_), a piece of fruit and a treat. Rarely did we eat school lunch although we often lobbied to eat it on pizza day. Dinners always included meat, vegetables, usually a starch and a glass of milk. We rarely had desert but me and my brothers would always find my dad's stash of amaretto cookies or our school treats and sneak some now and then.

Now college is where things fell apart even though, in retrospect, my diet before college wasn't all that great. My parents insisted on dorm living and school meal plans. I guess that was their way of making sure I was adequately housed and fed. Problem was that the meal plan I was on included all you can eat breakfast and dinner and allowed you to alternately eat at one of the campus restaurants that swapped out all you can eat for calories dense options like taco salads with deep fried shells and loaded with cheese, greasy grade B meat and sour cream with a coke as a drink. I took advantage of that from time to time but AYCE was my downfall. I imagine there were two forces working against me there my freshman year. First, I spent my entire childhood eating a lot because I was an athlete and constantly active. I had no concept of small portions because my body demanded more. Secondly, AYCE restaurants were associated with B-Day's growing up. Chinese buffets more specifically. I think there was some kind Pavlovian response going on there. AYCE = B-Day = Happy/reward. Sadly the reward was gaining 20lbs that first year (or was it semester?) Still. I went 6' 1.5 " 168lbs(if I remember) to 188lbs. Still relatively thin though. In my Sophomore year(or maybe it was the second half of my Freshman year) I believe I got off the meal plan or we lowered the "benefits". I recall ramen and hotdogs becoming staple foods in the dorms. I might have had milk but I am pretty sure that was replaced by canned sodas since I recall a can tower I had built with empty cans. They didn't drink themselves! Getting out of the dorms and off my meal plan made things a little more sane but I was now completely on my own paying for things unlike before. I don't recall my diet improving. I do recall $1 menus being big when I was working. Bean burritos at Taco Bell to be more specific or the all you can eat pasta and meat sauce at Fazolli's on Tuesday nights.

Married in 2000 and diet got a tad better than it had been but not by much. As I mentioned earlier - desk job + poor diet = 252 and an enlarged right ventricle. "Dieting/Lifestyle" has been an on and off thing even after moving too Paleo and more recently an organic Paleo diet. When I'm on I'm on and stay on the straight and narrow but if I give myself too much leeway I can easily get hooked on high reward foods especially during stressful times.

So here we are in the present. Epi-Paleo diet. 3 weeks in the bag I believe. Here is a typical day.

Breakfast within 30-40 min of waking - 6am

-   Can of skinned boneless Sardines in olive oil
-   Half pound of Australian ground lamb(ground myself from leg of lamb) or grass-fed beef. The lamb is not necessarily 100% pastured since it is from Australia and they do have times of the year when the sheep can't pasture.
-   16oz Coffee( drank over a 2-3 of hours) starting at 7 am
    -   At least 2tbs organic extra-virgin, unrefined, cold pressed coconut oil
    -   At least 2tbs homemade ghee from Kerry Gold unsalted
    -   .5-.75tps curcumin (95% curcumin)
    -   300mg theanine
    -   Usually a decent amount of cinnamon
-   Supplements/Vitamins
    -   1 pill PrescriptAssist probiotic
    -   1 pill Life Extensions Super K with Advanced K2 Complex
    -   2000mg Vitamin C
    -   200mg magnesium bisglycinate
    -   200mg selenium
    -   1 pill Pure Encapsulations B-Comlex Plus
    -   500mg Niacin
    -   5g Glutamine
    -   5g Creatine
    -   1 Now Foods Super Enzymes(Betaine HCL, ox Bile Extract, Papaya Fruit powder, Pancreatin 10x(Amylase, protease, Lipase), Bromelain, Papain, Cellulase, pepsin
    -   Now Foods Betaine HCL w/pepsin(I've tried all kinds of dosing here from 2 pills to 6. Can't really tell where I top out.)
-   Use to take until very recently
    -   1000mg L-Tyrosine
    -   1000mg Tryptophan
    -   500mg L-Carnetine

10am -

-   2 pills Gaia Thyroid Support(moved from morning dosing)
-   Used to take until very recently
    -   250mg Rhodiola Rosea
    -   600mg Alpha GPC

Lunch around 11:00/12:00 - This varies and lately haven't been that hungry at lunch so I wait for dinner

-   Half pound of ground beef or lamb (like breakfast) or even shrimp
-   Big plate of greens - steamed kale/shard from local organic farmer or organic spring mix
-   scoop of coconut oil and/or ghee
-   16oz of goat bone broth(sometimes beef)
-   2 cups of coconut/goat kefir
-   Sometimes a large serving of organic sauerkraut
-   1 Now Foods Super Enzymes(Betaine HCL, ox Bile Extract, Papaya Fruit powder, Pancreatin 10x(Amylase, protease, Lipase), Bromelain, Papain, Cellulase, pepsin
-   Now Foods Betaine HCL w/pepsin(I've tried all kinds of dosing here from 2 pills to 6. Can't really tell where I top out.)

4pm

-   1 pills Gaia Thyroid Support
-   Used to take until very recently
    -   250mg Rhodiola Rosea
    -   600mg Alpha GPC

Dinner around 4:00-5:00 - meals varies

-   Wild Atlantic salmon fillet/half pound shrimp
-   Half pound ground beef(sometimes substitute with an extra fish fillet)
-   Big plate of broccoli or Normandy vegetables or something along those lines like Kale and/or chard
-   More coconut oil and or ghee
-   Trying to get more carbs in like a sweet potato since I am very low carb
-   Sometimes a large serving of organic sauerkraut
-   2 cups of coconut/goat kefir
-   1 Now Foods Super Enzymes(Betaine HCL, Ox Bile Extract, Papaya Fruit powder, Pancreatin 10x(Amylase, protease, Lipase), Bromelain, Papain, Cellulase, pepsin
-   Now Foods Betaine HCL w/pepsin(I've tried all kinds of dosing here from 2 pills to 6. Can't really tell where I top out.)

6pm - Used to workout until recently

Bed time 9:00-10:00 usually

-   200mg magnesium bisglycinate
-   30mg Zinc

I try to stick to this as best I can. Breakfast is pretty much set. That doesn't vary much. Sometimes I throw some kraut in there. Lunch, as I said, varies but not too wildly. Recently, I've been skipping lunch unless I am feeling really hungry. I'm not sure if this is just my body changing or the fatty coffee over 2-3 hours is still producing satiety. I tend to go with that explanation. Now dinner is where I am having issues. I eat quite a bit but 4-5 hours later I am feeling hungry. I just ignore it an go to sleep but I am wondering what the deal might be here. This happens even when I eat lunch. I though maybe I was damaging my stomach in some way with the 6 pills of betaine HCL and that was causing what felt like hunger pains so I reduced the amount to 2 pills per meal but things haven't changed. Besides I typically feel a stomach ache when I have overdone the HCL in the past.

Weight has started to come off but not much. It also seems to correlate with eating 2 meals instead of 3 per day. Regardless if I eat 2 or 3 meals a day I end up feeling hungry by the time bed rolls around 4-5 hours later.

To Be Continued...............

### FutureVision — 2015-09-17T02:17:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/diary-i-mean-journal-of-a-madman.16871/post-177255>

**Look inside yourself Simba. You are more than what you have become**
Enough with the surface stuff. Time to dig deep. I've have bloodwork done in December in 2014(when I started to see a new naturopath) and again in June 2015. I've had a food sensitivity test(BioTek Standard Food Panel : IgG - Dec 2014), a stool test(Genova Diagnostics - Comprehensive Digestive Stool Analysis Dec 2014), and a SIBO test(Gut-Chek June 2014). I'm not really sure the best way to post my results here since I know some of the tests were probably not very useful but I'll do my best. Let's review the one off tests first.

**BioTek Standard Food Panel : IgG - Dec 2014**
Sensitivities to:

-   Casein (III)
-   Cheese, Cottage (III almost IV) Cheddar (I), Mozarella(II)
-   Milk(Cow) (III almost IV)
-   Whey (IV)
-   Yogurt (III)
-   Goat Milk (0) - Seems fine
-   Chicken Eggs (IV)
-   Duck Eggs (I)
-   Beef, lamb, pork, turkey chicken meat - (0)
-   Bakers Yeast (IV), Brewer's Yeast(III)
-   Cranberry(III)
-   Banana and pineapple(close to one I)
-   Fish/Crustacea/Mollusk - nada
-   Almonds (I)
-   Kidney Beans (II)
-   String Beans (almost a I)
-   Sesame Seeds (II almost III)
-   Mushrooms (III)
-   Radish (I)

Nothing in the very high - extremely high range.
\----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
**Genova Diagnostics - Comprehensive Digestive Stool Analysis Dec 2014**

-   Digestion
    -   Chymotrypsin - 18.0
    -   Putrefactive SCFAs (Total\*) - 6.0
    -   No meat fibers
    -   Few vegetable fibers
-   Metabolic markers
    -   Beneficial SCFAs (Total\*) - 34.0
    -   n-Butyrate - 10.4
    -   Beta-Glucuronidase - 3.116
    -   pH - 6.8

### FutureVision — 2015-09-18T15:47:42+0300

One of the things I noticed recently is that I am getting tired really early in the night. Typically around 8pm. My first thought was that maybe the reduction in blue light might be the cause of this early sleepiness. My second thought is that I've mentally been much more engaged over the last few weeks worth reading Dr. K''s book., starting the 4 Passes of Water, reading and participating in many FB discussions and reading blogs. There has certainly been an increased level of stress trying to find answers and piecing all of the information together. Stress dose tire me out. There are other stressors too with family and helping them in their path to optimal but there is resistance. Working on just letting go and focusing on my journey no matter how crazy I may seem at times. My last thought was I am simply too low carb. Other than vegetables I have not really had any carbs. I did eat two small see potatoes yesterday (one with lunch and one with dinner) and that seethed to help. Didn't feel as crappy as yesterday morning but still woke up in the middle of the night for a trip too the bathroom and couldn't get back too sleep. This was around 4:29 this morning.

Any feedback would be appreciated.

### Deverie_Night Owl — 2015-09-22T01:07:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/diary-i-mean-journal-of-a-madman.16871/post-177527>

Hello and welcome! Thanks for the interesting read.

I think it's totally normal to start feeling more sleepy earlier in the evening when doing the leptin reset. I couldn't keep my eyes open the first week, it was ridiculous. My husband wasn't sure what to think of me!

I think your diet looks fine. Eating seafood 1-3 times per day is excellent! I think people have recommended that if you're eating some carbs, eat them earlier in the day, not at dinner. Try cauliflower and broccoli vs. sweet potatoes at this time.

Are you doing any CT? That might be a good thing for you to add. Start with face dunks for two weeks to cold adapt!

Are you getting AM sunlight? You need this to yoke your circadian rhythm with your BAB.

I wouldn't worry about doing more labs at this time. Looks like you have a lot to start with. You could also post your labs in the Optimal Labs section and people could hack them there. Like I was told when I first started, labs are a "snapshot in time" and are not always what we need to look at. Go with how you feel and how your body is performing. (It took me a long time to figure that out - I thought for SURE I needed some numbers! I couldn't bear the thought that I actually had to evaluate how my body was FEELING.)

I would drop all supplements for a little while and add them back in if you really need them. Taking HCl can mess up your digestion - if you take it too long, your stomach will stop making it. I have a protocol that I give out to my patients if we determine they are low on stomach acid. Basically, you use the supplement version to encourage your stomach to make its own HCl, then you stop taking it. It should not be used long term. Let me know if you want the protocol.

A standing desk has benefits and drawbacks. Be sure when you are standing that you don't lock your knees. I recommend standing and sitting during the day, mix it up so you don't get stiff. Is your desk adjustable?

Yes, making all these changes can certainly be stressful! It affects everything, your schedule, your relationships, your work. But just keep at it. It took me a few months to get into the swing of it, and I'm not fully there yet. But sticking with the basics really eases the stress - morning sunlight, grounding, lots of water, lots of DHA!!

Glad you're here!

### FutureVision — 2015-09-22T16:31:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/diary-i-mean-journal-of-a-madman.16871/post-177569>

Now, leptin resistance. My understanding is that leptin, as the master hormone, needs to be optimized first otherwise focusing on fixing my other issues will not be very fruitful. For that to happen I need to optimize my environment inside(gut) and out. I have labs that lead my naturopath to believe that I have a gut issue and thyroid issue. I'm learning that my low vitamin D is probably not an absorption issue linked too poor gut absorption but a light issue. I've taken steps to get morning UV the past week or 2 and I have been out sitting in the sun around 1pm everyday the sun has been out for over a month now. I'm also trying to stay out of all artificial light whenever possible and I'm almost always running f.lux on my PC's and Twilight on my phone at or near the most red settings. Uvex glasses go on at sundown and even in the morning until I get a chance to get outside and look at the blue sky. I say the blue sky because I am up at 6 am everyday and the sun has just barely started to light up the sky this time of year. The sun is also out of sight behind the trees where I live until later in the morning. Not sure if that matters or not.

My sluggish thyroid sounds like it is also tied back to leptin and maybe even an autoimmune issue although it doesn't seem to present itself that way(or maybe it does). So here is my quandry. I don't really know if I am actually leptin resistant. I'm 228lbs and fairly muscular. At 15% body fat(as an ideal weight) I am right at being 30lbs overweight and have been stuck here even after starting the Leptin RX about a month ago. I don't think I have the necessary labs to tell for sure and I haven't done labs in 3 months so getting those done now seems prudent especially since I've made significant changes in lifestyle over the last month. I'm just not positive which ones to get.

I guess ultimately I am trying to find point A on my health map so I know what direction I need to go to get to point B.

### FutureVision — 2015-09-22T19:26:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/diary-i-mean-journal-of-a-madman.16871/post-177576>

> [Deverie\_Night Owl said:](/goto/post?id=177527)
>
> Are you doing any CT? That might be a good thing for you to add. Start with face dunks for two weeks to cold adapt!

Hi Deverie. Thanks for the welcome. I just started CT. I tried the face dunk last week and wasn't too thrilled by it. A few days ago I oped to just get in the tub and slowly fill it with cold water(no ice) as high as it would go. I managed to get the water high enough to cover my clavical and upper back. Legs were sticking out though. Water was roughly 65-69 degrees but it certainly felt pretty cold. Yesterday I was out with just shorts and undershirt with a short sleeve button down over it in 43 degree weather for about 30 minutes. Later in the early evening i id the tub thing again except this time the water was 65. I was definitely sivering on and off. I then went into my office where I had the AC set to 60 degrees to get it as cold as I could in there. Today, I am in my office with shorts and not shirt or anything on my feet. I turned my portable AC unit to face directly at me and have it on high and set to 60 degrees. The air was hitting me at about 63 degrees. I probably stood there for close to an hour then I switched computers so my back was facing the AC instead. In fact I am sitting here as I type with the air on me. Definitely shivering again. Lookingforward to go warming up in the sun in about 30 minutes.

One thing I've noticed as I have shifted to sleeping with only a sheet on at night and the temp set to 68(although it's starting to get colder than that at night as the weather changes) is that my shoulder and hip start bothering me. I am dealing with bursitis in thehip and I imagine the shoulder isn't much different. Maybe tendonitis. Whatever it is when I get cold enough they act up and I can't sleep.

([http://www.prnewswire.com/news-rele...roid-disease-remain-undiagnosed-73936867.html](http://www.prnewswire.com/news-releases/over-13-million-americans-with-thyroid-disease-remain-undiagnosed-73936867.html)).

I obviously don't have congenital hypothyroidism(which that thread you suggested is about) but it looks like, according to that thread, the RX for my situation is the same.

### FutureVision — 2015-09-28T21:35:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/diary-i-mean-journal-of-a-madman.16871/post-178052>

Ah. Yes. Water. So here is what I typically drink besides the 16.7oz water I use for my coffee. Most of the water I use/drink is from Costco or Sams' Club. Both are reverse osmosis purified but they do add back in some minerals like calcium chloride or sodium birobonate. Other than that I'm drinking at least one 25.3oz Pelligrino a day in addition to anywhere from 2-3 of the 16.9 oz bottles of RO water. I'm looking into springs in the area. There is one local mountain spring water bottler but I don't think their process is optimal. From their site:

All spring water passes through activated charcoal and sediment filters. Then an ozonation process takes place to kill any potential bacteria that could be present. The water is then stored in a stainless steel silo ready for bottling.

[https://www.google.com/url?sa=t&sou...YG3A3M&usg=AFQjCNFHPPG1yrq5Fz2BtoqVN9S\_mIOOFg](https://www.google.com/url?sa=t&source=web&rct=j&url=https://www.jackkruse.com/ubiquitination-17-gears-of-the-eye-clock/&ved=0CBwQFjAAahUKEwjQhKTwsJrIAhXPNIgKHYG3A3M&usg=AFQjCNFHPPG1yrq5Fz2BtoqVN9S_mIOOFg)

### Jack Kruse — 2015-09-29T02:23:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/diary-i-mean-journal-of-a-madman.16871/post-178079>

Psoriasis needs full spectrum UVB/UVA/IR and lots of spring water due to sodium bicarb for its power in the eye clock and on ocular melatonin generation.
Bicarbonate alters how we see and how the eye clock functions by modifying the visual signal generated by rod and [cone photoreceptors](http://phys.org/tags/cone+photoreceptors/) that detect light. The RPE controls the recycling and health of both rods and cones in different parts of the day.
Once lost, rods and cones are not replaced in humans, so an irreversible blindness is the outcome and a life time of circadian diseases that all tie back to epigenetic changes induced by cGMP mechanisms.
**Bicarb increases cGMP and this helps light transduction, especially UV light transduction.**

### Joe Gavin — 2015-09-29T16:41:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/diary-i-mean-journal-of-a-madman.16871/post-178131>

Caught her messing around with another man....

Coffee is a hack to get you going. Procaffenating according to Jack is sign of poor redox as well. Poor redox is a sign of poor mito functioning. It also can be dehydrating and demineraling in a high nnEMF environment. As your mitochondria become more effecient you shouldn't need the hack. I quit ita couple of months ago based on some stuff in the FB group and as a self-test. Haven't missed it really.

I do feel more hydrated. I work in a horrible nnEMF workplace so I still slam a ton of water. Just started the reptile light (UVA/UVB) yesterday at work and can report I felt great when i left for the day and still had good energy when I got home more than I had previously. Incredible really. I live in NJ so I need the light!

### FutureVision — 2015-09-30T20:34:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/diary-i-mean-journal-of-a-madman.16871/post-178303>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=178079)
>
> Psoriasis needs full spectrum UVB/UVA/IR and lots of spring water due to sodium bicarb for its power in the eye clock and on ocular melatonin generation.
> Bicarbonate alters how we see and how the eye clock functions by modifying the visual signal generated by rod and [cone photoreceptors](http://phys.org/tags/cone+photoreceptors/) that detect light. The RPE controls the recycling and health of both rods and cones in different parts of the day.
> Once lost, rods and cones are not replaced in humans, so an irreversible blindness is the outcome and a life time of circadian diseases that all tie back to epigenetic changes induced by cGMP mechanisms.
> **Bicarb increases cGMP and this helps light transduction, especially UV light transduction.**

From the light standpoint only using NB-UVB lamp would not be optimal for overall health. I would be better off with a mercury vapor bulb.
Does a NB-UVB lamp have any place in my arsenal?

**Niels Ryberg Finsen** (December 15, 1860 – September 24, 1904) was a [Faroese](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Faroe_Islands)\-[Danish](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Denmark) [physician](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Physician) and [scientist](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Scientist) of [Icelandic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Iceland) descent. He was awarded the [Nobel Prize](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nobel_Prize) in [Medicine and Physiology](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nobel_Prize_in_Physiology_or_Medicine) in 1903 "in recognition of his contribution to the treatment of diseases, especially [lupus vulgaris](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lupus_vulgaris), **with concentrated [light radiation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Light_therapy)**, whereby he has opened a new avenue for medical science."

### JanSz — 2015-10-07T23:03:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/diary-i-mean-journal-of-a-madman.16871/post-178839>

**Exo Terra Solar Glo 160W**
is meant as all in one high intensity light (for lizards).
It have enough power that you can keep one light at 2-3 feet distance from your head and it should make some difference.
Two or three of these lamps would probably replicate mid USA on sunny day.
(**On strong sunny day in middle of USA one may get 150 μW/cm^2 UVB)**
but not tropical sunlight.

In the winter time it would help with heating bill.
In the summer it would increase AC bill.

This light arrangement when run at about 14-15 hr/day could then replicate summer day in mid America.
Those who experience SAD could run it 365/year and likely improve their Circadian Rhythm.

I assume that when one runs 14 or 15hrs day, during that "day time" he would not wear anything, no blue blockers, no clothes.
Blue light from computer screen is less (or no) harmful during "day time".
Blue light (from any source) is harmful after sundown.
\------
The next best light that I know of is reptile UVB200.
One would need multitude of UVB200 lamps to get **150 μW/cm^2 UVB at 3 feet distance,**
That lamp does not produce heat, it gives off white light somewhat resembling deser light.

Checking in because this last week has been crappy in the sleep department. Most nights in up 2 or 3 times to hit the bathroom. It's not like I drink a lot of fluid before bed but it is something fairly new since I started my optimal journey. Coupled with that I'm am very stiff in the morning too the point of pain. This is mostly in my lower back but I'll feel it in my shoulder/upper back and hip too. As I've mentioned before I've had back issues for some time. The first time I had it checked out years ago they said I had degenerative arthritis in my upper and lower back. A few years late xrays hosed nothing. About a year and a half ago I had my hip checked out. That showed bursitis and some microtears. The back looked fine. So I'm wondering what is up. The only changes I made recently was cutting out coffee and eating more 90-100% dark chocolate. I do have a sensitivity to dairy but I don't think there is much if any in that high of a percentage. If I understand this correctly I could react to the proteins much like gluten. Maybe? The only other thing that is out of the ordinary would be my kids have taken turns being sick over the last 2 weeks. I haven't had any of their issues although I did have a nagging headache all day Sunday.

### FutureVision — 2015-10-15T20:30:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/diary-i-mean-journal-of-a-madman.16871/post-179600>

Back working on getting my CT on. A few weeks back I tried to go right into the croak soak. I wantedto do it for a bit but then hit a spell where I just didn't want to embrace the cold. Not even the air outside. Very odd to be turned off to it like that. I do find if I am tired, stressed or sick that the cold is not particularly attractive but none of that was going on at the time. Anyways I'm back at it. I tried face dunks the beginningof the week andI've come to the conclusion that is just not for me. I think it is this thing will holding my breath. I opted to go to bags of ice on the torso.

Yesterday I laid on the bare cellar floor(already cold) and placed 2 15lb bags of ice on my upper body(compression shirt on. Nike Pro Combat long sleeve to be specific). One across my chest and the other right below it across my midsection. turned Spotify on and listend to Chris Cornell's new album and did 10 minutes and got the skin down to 60.

Today I did the same as yesterday but after the first 10 minutes(skin down to 56 this time). I laid out a T shape with the bags of ice directly on the floor and laid on them. Head propped with a pillow. Definitely got a shiver on with that since my body weight was adding extra compression. Quick scan on the skin and I was right around the 55 mark. Win!

Started thinking about how I might build an ice coffin of sorts for these CT sessions or a ice cross to lay on. Of course by the time I work that coffin out I might just be ready to move into a tank providing I can find the resources to get one.

I will say there is one challenge with all of these session. Pounding 32 oz of ice cold water. Even an hour after eating my BAB it's tough to get that much water down in a short period of time but I get it done.

That's all for today folks. Stay tuned.

### FutureVision — 2015-10-28T19:31:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/diary-i-mean-journal-of-a-madman.16871/post-180636>

I was just thinking about how we tend to look at things. Do I look at the glass as being half empty or being half full? Honestly, I look at my glass as having a big ole crack in it and I can't hope to keep any water in it until it is fixed. That's kind of where I am at today. My recent labs have shown that I am definitely not optimal and if anything things have gotten worse over the last few months since I switched over to the Leptin RX. I'm not saying that is the cause. I may have already, been trending in the wrong direction for awhile. It's just that I never expected my recent labs to show almost no improvement and in fact a degredation in my condition. TSH increased(slightly), D3 took a crap, and a number of other markers that were low including testosterone. Still got more labs coming but honestly I'm not expecting better news.

So here I am with that big ole crack staring me in the face and so far duct tape and super glue haven't worked. Time to try the blast furnace. Just got to figure out how to get one and how to operate it so I can fix the crack. Heck, I'll make the glass even bigger so I can fill it up more! Maybe change the color too. Purple? Red?

### FutureVision — 2015-11-12T02:53:43+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/diary-i-mean-journal-of-a-madman.16871/post-181720>

Man! I come here to post in my journal and always end up reading everyone else's or posting on my other forum threads or ending up on Facebook. Probably doesn't help that it was a low UV day here in NH. I don't get as down as I used to and I think that has a lot to do with my diet changes and getting more sun. I couldn't be happier about that. It's really great to get up in the morning and not be able to tell what the weather is like outside by the way I feel.

Positve #2 is that the scale is moving. No real changes on my end other than maybe some carbs at night in the form of few ounces of canned pumpkin along with my meal. Sweet potatoes on deck. I'm hoping that this recent weight loss means I'm getting back to be leptin sensitive.

Speaking of being leptin sensitive I looked over the signs it is working.

-   Change in your sweating pattern
-   Energy levels will rise
-   Hunger and cravings will become less and eventually disappear
-   When you awaken you will feel refreshed and well rested

Change in sweating pattern is a tough one to figure out. Not really hot enough to sweat and I'm not working out so this one is a mystery to me still.

Energy levels are pretty good. I can't say I have boundless energy and the energy wanes as the sun goes down but I think that is probably normal

Hunger is getting better. I'd wake up hungry but now it's only a slight twinge. I'm also able to skip lunch with relative ease. I'll get a little hunger pang but it isn't so much in the form of a growling stomach but more a mental thing if that makes any sense. It might be nothing more that lingering signs of anticipation from eating lunch regularly. I tend to think this is the case.

I can't say I feel refreshed and well rested. I still wake up feeling crappy often. Mostly, stiff, achey and a bit foggy headed but I can say that is passes pretty quick. I'm sure my environment isn't helping with this living in a townhome with everyone's wifi on including my own(not by choice). All the bedrooms are on the second floor too.

And as I mentioned before there is weight loss. It's not fast but then again I guess we need to define fast. I'd say 5-7 lbs over the last week and a half is pretty fast. I thought it might be dehydration and I suppose that is possible since I've spent a lot more time in my office the ast few days but I've probably drank more water than I usually do. I kno because I'm always bringing down 2-4 RO water bottles and a couple 25oz Pellegrino bottles each day.

So a number of things are going through my head now. Are these results for real? Are they even possible with my labs looking pretty out of whack? I'm borderline/definite hypothyroid depending on which range you use. Is it possible to lose weight in that situation? Difficultly losing weight, as a symptom, seems to imply that it is not impossible. Maybe it is a sign my thyroid is starting to get sorted out since my other labs in mid October. I sure hope so.

I know my journey feels like it is going nowhere at times andother times I feel like I am chasing my own tail but I believe I am heading in the right direction and that is what counts. That's tough for someone to admit who is very results focused. I used to blame it on my ADD but I am pretty sure that is a dopamine thing now.

### FutureVision — 2015-11-12T03:19:26+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/diary-i-mean-journal-of-a-madman.16871/post-181724>

Thought I might add in some questions I've been asking myself.

1.  How can I do a road trip down to FL and meet up with other quantum folks along the way? I have the freedom with the work I do since all I really need is a laptop and an internet connection and occasionally my phone. Problem is dinero.

2.  Am I getting too mired in getting all my labs done? Would my limited financial resources be better spent on a better option?
3.  What the hell should I be focused on reading/listening too? Blogs? Forum? Books? Webinars? Q&A's? There is so much to read and there feels like no direction. Actually, I guess all of it is in the right direction but it seems very random. I've read Fourth Phase of Water, The Monk Who Sold His Ferrari, The Body Electric and part A of Life in Shaping Life. I've been through blogs here and there but haven't completely any one series yet. I've been listening to the webinars and Q&A's and those always seem to lead me back to reading books and blogs
4.  Is all of this I am doing, the labs, the supplements, the food/water doing anyone any good other than myself? It's a strain on the finances. It's caused conflicts with the ones I love. It may very well be consuming to the point that it takes away from the things that are important. Of course, if I am dead or ill I do no one any good. I'm sincerely hoping that I'll see a trend in the right direction over the coming months to know for sure it is worth it. So far it seems like it is helping but I don't want that be a figment of my imagination. I want to see results and I guess I want those around me to see those results since they may be more objective than me.

FIN

Just dawned on me this morning during my sundream(Facebook ref) today that I am 3 months into my Leptin reset. I've seen some weight come off. I was 227 when I started this journal on 9/16 and I believe I was around 230-232 when I started in mid-August. I was as low as 220 3-4 days ago but I'm back up around 222-225. Funny how I get fixated on that. It's not really as important as the other things going on in my body is it? Psoriasis should be far more of a concern. The weight, like the psoriasis plaque, like any other physical manifestation if just a symptom of something deeper and at the core. Got to hang onto that train of though more tightly than anything else and work towards fixing the unseen. When I fix the unseen the seen with resolve. Maybe that is not true for everything but it seems like it is a paradigm that holds a lot of truth.
